proposals.title: Filtering Phylogenetic Noise: A Signal Processing Approach to Gene Order Analysis in Molluscan Mitochondrial Genomes
proposals.motivation: Phylogenetic reconstruction using mitochondrial gene order is often confounded by frequent, small-scale rearrangements, which can obscure deeper evolutionary relationships. Current methods treat all rearrangements equally, potentially leading to inaccurate trees in lineages with high rearrangement rates, such as gastropods. This project addresses the need for a method to distinguish phylogenetically informative, large-scale structural changes from high-frequency 'noise'.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a signal-processing-inspired filter to suppress the signal from frequent, small-scale mitochondrial genome rearrangements will yield more accurate and robust phylogenetic trees than analyses using raw gene order data.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline that treats gene order across taxa as a signal in 'genome space'. Using simulated genomes with known rates of macro- and micro-rearrangements, we will design and test kernel-based filters (e.g., low-pass) to identify and dampen high-frequency rearrangement signals. The filtered data will be analyzed using established distance-based (e.g., breakpoint distance) and adjacency-based phylogenetic methods, and tree accuracy will be compared against raw-data trees. The optimized filter will then be applied to empirical data from the Potamopyrgus system, leveraging its well-characterized mitochondrial architecture and rearrangement dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for gene order phylogenetics that explicitly models rearrangement rate heterogeneity. It will deliver a tested software tool for filtering gene order data and offer new insights into the phylogenetic utility of mitochondrial architecture in complex, rearrangement-prone lineages like Potamopyrgus.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its conceptual translation of signal processing theory to gene order evolution and in its explicit algorithmic separation of rearrangement classes for phylogenetic inference, moving beyond the assumption of uniform evolutionary signal across all structural variants.

proposals.title: Synteny and Developmental Co-expression as a Dual Signal for Resolving Phylogeny in the Potamopyrgus Species Complex
proposals.motivation: Phylogenies of closely related species, especially those with recent whole-genome duplications and complex reproductive modes like the New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum, are often difficult to resolve. While synteny of conserved gene blocks is a recognized phylogenetic character, its evolutionary stability and functional significance remain unclear. Integrating functional genomic data, such as developmental expression, could reveal whether synteny reflects conserved regulatory landscapes, thereby strengthening its phylogenetic utility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that phylogenies constructed from syntenic blocks will be more robust and congruent with established species relationships when those blocks also show conserved, stage-specific co-expression patterns, indicating that evolutionary rearrangements disrupting synteny are often accompanied by changes in developmental gene regulation.
proposals.method: We will generate chromosome-level genome assemblies for P. antipodarum and its close relatives (e.g., P. estuarinus, P. kaitunuparaoa) using Hi-C and PacBio/Oxford Nanopore long-read sequencing. Stage- and tissue-specific transcriptomes (including larval stages and sensory tissues) will be generated via RNA-seq. Synteny networks will be constructed using SyRI or D-GENIES, and phylogenies will be built from synteny matrices. Co-expression networks (using WGCNA) of syntenic gene sets will be analyzed across developmental stages and compared to phylogenetic signal strength.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, functionally informed framework for using genome architecture in phylogenetics. It will produce high-quality genomic and transcriptomic resources for a key model in sexual reproduction research. The findings will test if developmental constraint underpins synteny conservation, offering new insights into the interplay between genome rearrangement and gene regulation evolution.
proposals.novelty: While synteny and expression are studied separately, their combined analysis as a phylogenetic character is novel. Applying this integrated approach to a recent, dynamic species complex with documented whole-genome duplication and mixed reproductive modes is unprecedented and leverages Dr. Boore's established system.

proposals.title: Network Analysis of Dispersed Repeat Dynamics in Animal Mitochondrial Genomes: A Case Study in Potamopyrgus
proposals.motivation: Recent long-read sequencing of the Potamopyrgus antipodarum mitochondrial genome revealed a complex, chloroplast-like architecture with extensive repeat-mediated recombination, challenging the canonical view of animal mitochondrial evolution. Understanding the evolutionary dynamics and structural impact of these dispersed repeats is crucial, as they may drive genomic instability, influence mutation rates, and contribute to the maintenance of sexual reproduction. Pre-2026 methods for repeat analysis are often linear or pairwise, lacking a framework to model the complex, interdependent relationships among multiple repeat families across a genome.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dispersed repeats in non-canonical animal mitochondrial genomes form interconnected networks whose topological properties (e.g., centrality, modularity) correlate with recombination frequency, sequence divergence, and evolutionary age, and that these network structures differ significantly between sexual and asexual Potamopyrgus lineages.
proposals.method: We will construct similarity networks from long-read assemblies of P. antipodarum and related species, where nodes represent individual repeat copies and edges represent significant sequence homology (using BLASTN with stringent e-value thresholds). Network properties will be quantified using graph theory metrics (e.g., degree distribution, betweenness centrality, community detection via the Louvain algorithm) via the NetworkX library. These topological features will be statistically correlated with empirical data on recombination rates (from population sequencing) and repeat divergence times (estimated from synonymous substitution rates). Comparative network analysis will be performed across sexual/asexual clades and against published plastid genomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, network-based framework for modeling repeat evolution in animal organelle genomes, moving beyond cataloging to predictive modeling of structural evolution. It will directly test whether repeat network architecture is a genomic signature linked to reproductive mode in Potamopyrgus, offering mechanistic insights into the genomic consequences of asexuality. The methodology will be a novel tool for the field of comparative genomics.
proposals.novelty: Applying social/system biology network theory to the evolution of mitochondrial genome repeats is a novel conceptual shift. The explicit integration of network topology with population-genetic parameters (recombination, divergence) to explain the maintenance of complex mitochondrial architectures in animals is unexplored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Engineering a Synthetic Chloroplast Genome to Reprogram Larval Gustatory and Olfactory Gene Networks in Potamopyrgus antipodarum
proposals.motivation: The discovery of chloroplast-like architecture and repeat-mediated recombination in animal mitochondrial genomes (Boore et al., pre-2026) challenges the traditional animal-plant organelle divide and suggests potential for functional integration. Simultaneously, the role of diet-derived metabolites in shaping sensory gene expression (e.g., Gr/Or genes) during larval development is poorly understood due to a lack of tools for precise, causal manipulation of metabolite production within a heterotrophic host.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that introducing a minimal synthetic chloroplast genome, engineered to produce specific metabolites or signaling molecules, into the asexual lineage of Potamopyrgus antipodarum will directly and selectively reprogram the expression of larval gustatory and olfactory receptor genes, altering sensory development.
proposals.method: We will first design a minimal synthetic chloroplast genome using known plastid sequences, focusing on core metabolic pathways (e.g., isoprenoid biosynthesis). This construct will be delivered into P. antipodarum embryos via microinjection. We will then perform whole-genome sequencing to confirm integration and whole-transcriptome sequencing (RNA-seq) on larval stages, comparing engineered and wild-type asexual lineages. Differential expression analysis (using DESeq2) will identify perturbed gene networks, with a focus on chemosensory gene families (Gr/Or) annotated from the published P. antipodarum genome.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish P. antipodarum as a novel model for synthetic organelle biology, providing the first causal evidence for plastid-derived metabolites actively shaping animal sensory gene regulation. It will deliver a toolkit for organelle engineering in a metazoan and reveal fundamental principles of how metabolic symbiosis can influence neural development.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in fusing synthetic chloroplast engineering with animal developmental transcriptomics, using a non-plant, whole-genome duplication model to test the functional plasticity of organelle genomes in reprogramming heterotrophic physiology.

proposals.title: Resolving the Ancestral Animal Genome: Testing the Convergence vs. Conservation Hypothesis for Spiralian-Deuterostome Synteny Using Long-Read Sequencing and Phylogenomic Analysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 genomic studies reveal unexpected similarities in gene order and content between spiralians (e.g., mollusks like Potamopyrgus) and deuterostomes, which are often interpreted as deeply conserved ancestral bilaterian features. However, the alternative—that these similarities result from convergent evolution or independent gene loss in the intervening ecdysozoan lineage (e.g., arthropods, nematodes)—remains a critical, untested hypothesis. Resolving this is fundamental to understanding early animal genome evolution and the validity of using synteny as a deep phylogenetic signal.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed genomic similarities between spiralians and deuterostomes are not ancestral bilaterian traits but are instead the result of convergent evolutionary processes or widespread, independent gene loss within the ecdysozoan clade, making ecdysozoan genomes highly derived.
proposals.method: We will generate high-quality, chromosome-scale genome assemblies for key spiralian (including additional Potamopyrgus species and a platyhelminth) and deuterostome taxa using PacBio HiFi or Oxford Nanopore long-read sequencing and Hi-C scaffolding. We will employ rigorous synteny detection algorithms (e.g., SyRI, D-GENIES) to identify conserved genomic blocks. A novel, custom phylogenomic pipeline will then model the evolutionary history of these blocks using probabilistic methods (e.g., PhyloNet) to explicitly test between scenarios of ancestral conservation versus convergent gain/loss, incorporating models of genome rearrangement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive test of a major hypothesis in animal genome evolution, clarifying the genomic architecture of the last common bilaterian ancestor. It will establish a new analytical framework for distinguishing deep homology from convergence in comparative genomics. The resulting high-quality genomes will be a lasting resource for evolutionary and ecological genetics.
proposals.novelty: Previous work has catalogued similarities but has not rigorously modeled the alternative convergent evolution/loss hypothesis using explicit phylogenetic methods on whole-genome architecture. Leveraging the unique genomic features of the studied Potamopyrgus system—including its whole-genome duplication and variation in reproductive mode—provides a powerful within-spiralian control for testing rates of genomic change.

proposals.title: Ultra-Conserved Non-Coding Elements in Spiralians: Identification, Evolutionary Dynamics, and Functional Testing in Potamopyrgus antipodarum
proposals.motivation: Ultra-conserved non-coding elements (UCNEs) are critical for development and gene regulation in vertebrates, but their identification and functional significance in major invertebrate lineages like Spiralia remain poorly explored. The New Zealand mud snail, Potamopyrgus antipodarum, with its well-characterized genome, diverse reproductive modes, and status as a model for genome evolution, provides a unique system to investigate the deepest evolutionary conservation of regulatory architecture across bilaterians.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a core set of UCNEs, conserved between spiralians and deuterostomes, will be identifiable in the P. antipodarum genome and that these elements will be enriched near developmental regulator genes, with sequence variation correlating with phenotypic differences between sexual and asexual lineages.
proposals.method: We will first generate a high-quality chromosome-level genome assembly for a sexual P. antipodarum lineage using PacBio HiFi and Hi-C sequencing. Using phyloP and phastCons algorithms, we will perform multi-genome alignments across available spiralian (e.g., mollusks, annelids) and deuterostome genomes to identify deeply conserved non-coding sequences. Candidate UCNEs will be tested functionally using a dual approach: in vivo reporter assays in snail embryos via microinjection and in vitro enhancer assays in cell culture, assessing their regulatory potential on minimal promoters.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive map of bilaterian UCNEs in a spiralian model, directly linking deep sequence conservation to regulatory function. It will establish P. antipodarum as a functional genomics platform for spiralian biology and provide insights into how conserved regulatory landscapes evolve in the context of alternative reproductive strategies.
proposals.novelty: While UCNE studies are advanced in vertebrates, this proposal uniquely scales the search to the divergence of spiralians and deuterostomes (>550 million years) and leverages a novel molluscan system with exceptional genomic resources and intraspecific phenotypic variation for functional testing.

proposals.title: Functional Diversification of the Avirulence-like Superfamily in Potamopyrgus antipodarum: Linking Gene Duplication to Host-Parasite Dynamics
proposals.motivation: The New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum is a premier model for studying the maintenance of sexual reproduction, driven by coevolution with sterilizing trematode parasites. The recent discovery of a whole-genome duplication (WGD) in this species, coupled with the identification of an enormous superfamily of >700 avirulence-like (AVL) genes, presents a unique opportunity. Understanding the function of these genes is critical, as they are hypothesized to mediate host specificity and infection outcomes in this classic Red Queen host-parasite system, but their specific roles remain entirely unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the WGD-driven expansion of the AVL superfamily in P. antipodarum has led to functional diversification, with distinct subsets of genes conferring resistance or susceptibility to specific parasite lineages, thereby directly influencing the infection dynamics that maintain sexual reproduction.
proposals.method: We will leverage the existing whole-genome assemblies for sexual and asexual P. antipodarum lineages and related species. Using a combination of phylogenetic reconciliation (Notung, CAFE5) and selection pressure analysis (PAML, HyPhy), we will identify AVL gene families under positive selection. We will then perform targeted RNAi knockdowns in live snails, followed by controlled experimental challenges with the trematode Microphallus sp., to assay changes in infection prevalence and host sterility. Gene expression (RNA-seq) profiles of infected vs. uninfected snails will validate in vivo relevance.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly link a specific, massive gene family expansion to phenotypic outcomes in a canonical host-parasite model. It will provide the first functional evidence for the role of AVL genes in P. antipodarum, moving beyond genomic description to mechanistic understanding. The results will offer a novel genetic explanation for the variation in infection rates that underpin the evolutionary maintenance of sex.
proposals.novelty: While the AVL superfamily's expansion is documented, its functional analysis is unprecedented. Integrating comparative genomics from recent P. antipodarum studies with direct phenotypic assays in the live host-parasite system represents a novel, causal approach to understanding the genomic arms race.

proposals.title: Horizontal Gene Transfer from Phototrophs in the Asexual Lineages of Potamopyrgus antipodarum: Evidence from Comparative and Phylogenomic Analysis of Nuclear and Mitochondrial Genomes
proposals.motivation: Horizontal gene transfer (HGT) from phototrophs (e.g., algae, plants) to animals is a rare but potentially transformative evolutionary mechanism, offering novel metabolic capabilities. The New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum, with its well-characterized whole-genome duplication, diverse sexual and asexual lineages, and documented mitochondrial genome complexity, provides a powerful system to investigate such rare HGT events. Identifying phototroph-derived genes could reveal how asexual lineages overcome evolutionary constraints by acquiring exogenous genetic material.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that asexual lineages of P. antipodarum possess nuclear or mitochondrial genes acquired via horizontal transfer from phototrophic organisms, and that these genes are absent from closely related sexual lineages. These transfers may contribute to the ecological success and persistence of asexual clones.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing whole-genome assemblies for sexual and asexual P. antipodarum, P. estuarinus, and P. kaitunuparaoa. Using a custom pipeline integrating BLASTp, DIAMOND, and HGT-detection algorithms like HGTector, we will screen all predicted protein sequences against a comprehensive non-metazoan database (NCBI nr, Phytozome). Candidate HGTs will be validated through rigorous phylogenomic reconstruction (PhyloBayes, RAxML) to demonstrate a phototroph origin and animal recipient topology. We will also analyze the novel single-molecule mitochondrial genome assembly for evidence of chloroplast-like architectural features or integrated plastid-derived sequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evidence for or against phototroph-to-animal HGT within a model system for the evolution of sex. It will identify specific, candidate horizontally acquired genes, characterizing their genomic context and potential functional roles. The findings will directly inform debates on the sources of genetic novelty in asexual animal lineages and the limits of HGT in complex multicellular eukaryotes.
proposals.novelty: While HGT is known in some bdelloid rotifers and tardigrades, it is unexplored in P. antipodarum, despite its unique genomic resources. The explicit focus on phototroph donors and the integration of nuclear and highly complex mitochondrial genomic data within a comparative framework of sexual and asexual reproduction is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Gene Family Contraction and Mitochondrial Genome Reduction in the Evolution of Parasitism in Potamopyrgus Lineages
proposals.motivation: The transition to a parasitic lifestyle is a major evolutionary shift often accompanied by genomic reduction, but the specific patterns of gene family contraction in gastropods remain poorly characterized. Pre-2026 work on Potamopyrgus antipodarum, a model for sexual/asexual reproduction, provides a unique genomic foundation to investigate this. Studying gene loss in a comparative framework can reveal core metabolic and developmental pathways dispensable for parasitism, offering functional insights into host dependence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the evolution of parasitism in specific Potamopyrgus lineages is associated with the non-random contraction of gene families involved in nutrient biosynthesis, detoxification, and free-living environmental sensing, with parallel losses observable in the mitochondrial genome.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing whole-genome assemblies for sexual and asexual P. antipodarum and related species to perform comparative genomics. Gene families will be identified and clustered using OrthoFinder v2.5.4. Contraction/expansion events will be inferred using CAFE 5, mapped to a phylogeny incorporating parasitic and free-living lineages. We will specifically analyze the mitochondrial genome architecture using long-read data (as in Boore's prior single-molecule sequencing work) to detect repeat-mediated recombination and gene loss associated with parasitic taxa.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic catalog of gene family contractions associated with parasitism in a gastropod model system, linking nuclear and mitochondrial genome reduction. It will identify candidate pathways essential for a free-living state that are lost in parasites, advancing our understanding of genome evolution in host-dependent organisms.
proposals.novelty: While genome reduction in parasites is known, applying rigorous gene family evolution analysis (CAFE) to the well-characterized Potamopyrgus system—with its unique sexual/asexual dynamics and available high-quality genomes—is novel. Integrating nuclear gene family data with detailed mitochondrial architecture analysis from long-read sequencing provides a unique holistic genomic perspective.

proposals.title: Co-evolutionary arms races in Potamopyrgus: Genomic signatures of host resistance gene evolution in response to expanded pathogen gene families
proposals.motivation: The New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum is a premier model for studying the maintenance of sexual reproduction, largely attributed to Red Queen dynamics with co-evolving parasites. Pre-2026 genomic resources, including the whole-genome sequence and mitochondrial architecture of P. antipodarum, have revealed its complex evolutionary history but have not been leveraged to identify the specific host resistance genes under selection. Scaling analysis to host genomes is critical to directly test the genetic arms race hypothesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that gene families involved in pathogen recognition and immune response within the P. antipodarum genome will show signatures of rapid evolution and positive selection that are correlated with the expansion and diversity of known parasite gene families.
proposals.method: We will use the existing P. antipodarum reference genome and new PacBio HiFi assemblies from sexual and asexual lineages to annotate candidate immune and resistance gene families (e.g., TLRs, NLRs, lectins). We will then perform comparative genomic and phylogenomic analyses using tools like BUSTED and FUBAR from the HyPhy suite to detect pervasive positive selection. Co-evolutionary signatures will be assessed by correlating measures of host gene family expansion and diversification with known pathogen (e.g., Microsporidia) genome content across different snail populations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first genome-wide identification of candidate resistance genes in the P. antipodarum system, directly linking host genomic architecture to parasite-driven evolution. It will generate a concrete genomic framework for future studies on host-parasite co-evolution and the costs and benefits of sexual reproduction.
proposals.novelty: While the role of parasites in this system is well-established ecologically, the specific host genomic targets of this selection are unknown. This proposal moves beyond correlative population studies to a gene-family-centric analysis, integrating the unique genomic resources of this model to dissect the molecular interface of a classic co-evolutionary arms race.

proposals.title: Testing the Invariance of the 37-Gene Mitochondrial Complement Across the Acari: A Phylogenomic Survey of Mites and Ticks
proposals.motivation: The animal mitochondrial genome is often described as a highly conserved, compact molecule with a canonical set of 37 genes. This assumption underpins comparative genomics and phylogenetic studies, yet recent work in diverse invertebrates, including mollusks, has revealed surprising deviations in gene content and architecture. The hyper-diverse arachnid order Acari (mites and ticks) represents a critical but under-surveyed lineage for testing the universality of this model, given their ancient divergence and varied life histories.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the canonical set of 37 mitochondrial genes is not strictly conserved across all Acari species, and that gene losses, duplications, or transfers have occurred in specific lineages.
proposals.method: We will assemble and annotate mitochondrial genomes from a phylogenetically broad panel of Acari species, prioritizing taxa from understudied superfamilies. Building on Dr. Boore's expertise, we will utilize long-read single-molecule sequencing (PacBio or Nanopore) to overcome assembly challenges posed by repetitive regions and structural variation. Gene annotation will be performed using a customized pipeline combining MITOS2 with homology searches against a curated database of chelicerate mitochondrial genes, followed by manual curation. Phylogenetic comparative methods will be used to map any gene content changes onto a robust species tree.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive test of mitochondrial gene content conservation across the Acari, either solidifying a fundamental tenet of animal mitochondrial biology or revealing novel exceptions that challenge the canonical model. It will generate a high-quality genomic resource for an ecologically and medically important group and inform our understanding of the evolutionary forces shaping mitochondrial genome architecture.
proposals.novelty: While gene loss is known in some animal mtDNA, a systematic survey for deviations from the 37-gene set has not been conducted across the immense taxonomic and ecological diversity of the Acari. This project directly tests a widely held assumption in a lineage where it remains plausible but unverified.

proposals.title: Evolutionary and Mechanistic Drivers of Reversed GC-Skew in the Mitochondrial Genome of Dermatophagoides pteronyssinus
proposals.motivation: Strand-specific GC-skew is a fundamental, conserved feature of most animal mitochondrial genomes, linked to asymmetric mutation pressures during replication and transcription. The reversal of this skew in the house dust mite D. pteronyssinus represents a significant evolutionary anomaly with unknown functional consequences. Understanding its cause is crucial for testing models of mitochondrial genome evolution and may reveal novel mechanisms of replication or repair in compact, AT-rich genomes.
proposals.hypothesis: The reversed GC-skew in D. pteronyssinus mitochondria is driven by a fundamental shift in replication origin and strand-displacement dynamics, leading to altered mutational pressures, and is associated with specific changes in the amino acid composition of encoded proteins.
proposals.method: We will generate high-fidelity, long-read mitochondrial genome sequences (using PacBio or Nanopore technology, as in Boore's prior work) from D. pteronyssinus and related mite species to precisely map replication origins and termini. We will employ comparative genomic analyses to quantify substitution patterns and infer ancestral states. Strand-specific bioinformatics pipelines (e.g., skew plots, differential codon usage analysis) will be used to correlate skew patterns with gene orientation, transcription rates (inferred from RNA-seq), and protein physicochemical property changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed mechanistic hypothesis for reversed mitochondrial GC-skew in animals, linking genome architecture to molecular evolution. It will expand models of mitochondrial genome stability beyond typical bilaterian systems and contribute to understanding extreme genomic adaptations in arthropods.
proposals.novelty: While GC-skew is well-studied in standard models, a systematic investigation of its reversal leverages Boore's expertise in non-standard mitochondrial genomes to challenge a deep-seated genomic assumption. The integration of long-read sequencing for structural analysis with population-genetic inference of mutation bias is a novel synthesis for this problem.

proposals.title: Predicting Reproductive Mode and Habitat Salinity from Mitochondrial Genome Architecture in Potamopyrgus Snails
proposals.motivation: Mitochondrial gene order is known to be highly variable in some invertebrates, but its utility as a predictive tool for organismal traits remains unexplored. The New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum, with its well-characterized sexual/asexual lineages and brackish/freshwater habitat transitions, provides an ideal model system to test if mitochondrial genome rearrangements correlate with and can predict these key ecological and biological traits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific patterns of mitochondrial gene order and non-coding repeat content are not neutral but are statistically associated with, and can be used to predict, reproductive mode (sexual vs. asexual) and habitat salinity tolerance in Potamopyrgus.
proposals.method: We will compile a dataset of complete mitochondrial genomes from diverse P. antipodarum lineages (sexual/asexual, freshwater/estuarine) using existing data and new long-read sequencing. We will encode each genome as a feature vector representing gene adjacency, strand asymmetry, and repeat region architecture. Using supervised machine learning (e.g., Random Forest or Support Vector Machine classifiers), we will train models to predict reproductive mode and salinity niche from these structural features, validating predictions with known field and experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, genome-structure-based framework for predicting organismal traits, moving beyond sequence polymorphism. It will provide new, testable hypotheses on the potential adaptive or mechanistic links between mitochondrial genome architecture and fundamental life-history strategies in a key model system.
proposals.novelty: While mitochondrial gene order is used for phylogenetics, its inversion as a predictive tool for non-phylogenetic traits is novel. This approach directly tests the functional or correlative significance of genome rearrangements beyond their role as phylogenetic characters.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Evolutionary Irreversibility of Obligate Biotrophy: A Comparative Genomic Analysis of Facultative and Obligate Parasites in Potamopyrgus
proposals.motivation: The evolutionary trajectory from facultative to obligate biotrophy (e.g., parasitism) is widely considered a one-way, irreversible process leading to an evolutionary dead end, characterized by massive gene loss and host specialization. This paradigm, however, is largely based on comparisons between deeply divergent lineages, lacking a robust test within closely related taxa where transitions may be more recent and genomic signatures of potential reversibility could be detected. Pre-2026 genomic resources for the New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum, a model for reproductive mode evolution with known parasitic trematode interactions, provide a unique system to challenge this assumption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the evolutionary transition to obligate biotrophy is not universally irreversible; within closely related parasite lineages, we predict genomic evidence of retained or re-acquired metabolic functions, and in the host, evidence of co-evolutionary responses that could facilitate a return to facultative interactions under specific selective pressures.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing whole-genome sequences of P. antipodarum and its trematode parasites, alongside new long-read (PacBio/Oxford Nanopore) sequencing of additional facultative and obligate parasite strains co-occurring with sexual and asexual snail lineages. Using comparative genomics, we will construct high-quality pangenomes for both host and parasite clades. We will apply phylogenetic reconciliation methods (e.g., NOTUNG, GeneRax) to model gene family evolution, explicitly testing for patterns of gene loss, retention, and potential horizontal gene transfer. Co-evolutionary signatures will be analyzed using phylogenetic independent contrasts and tests for correlated evolutionary rates between host immune gene families and parasite effector genes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fine-scale genomic test of the 'evolutionary dead end' hypothesis within a closely related host-parasite system. It will generate a high-resolution dataset on co-evolutionary genomics in Potamopyrgus, linking reproductive mode (sexual/asexual) to parasite interaction evolution. The findings will refine models of parasite evolution and have implications for understanding the stability of specialized symbiotic interactions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a well-established molluscan model system for reproductive evolution to a fundamental question in parasite evolutionary biology, using pangenomic and co-evolutionary analyses on closely related lineages to detect subtle signals contradicting broad macroevolutionary patterns.

proposals.title: Comparative Genomic Analysis of Obligate and Hemibiotrophic Pathogens to Elucidate the Evolutionary Trajectory of Obligate Biotrophy
proposals.motivation: The evolution of obligate biotrophy, where pathogens can only survive on living host tissue, represents a major ecological and genomic transition that constrains pathogen life history and impacts disease management. Pre-2026 studies on fungi and oomycetes have identified gene loss, particularly in metabolic and toxin synthesis pathways, as a potential driver, but a systematic, genome-wide comparison across closely related species pairs is lacking. Understanding the conserved genomic signatures of this transition is crucial for predicting pathogen evolution and identifying novel, durable resistance targets in crops.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the transition from hemibiotrophy to obligate biotrophy is driven by convergent patterns of gene family contraction, pseudogenization, and structural genome simplification, particularly in nutrient assimilation and host cell death-inducing effector genes, rather than by the acquisition of novel genetic material.
proposals.method: We will select well-annotated genome pairs of closely related obligate and hemibiotrophic pathogens (e.g., from the genera *Albugo*, *Phytophthora*, or rust fungi). Using a custom pipeline integrating OrthoFinder for orthogroup inference and CAFE5 for gene family evolution analysis, we will identify significantly contracted/expanded families. We will perform synteny analysis using MCScanX to detect structural rearrangements and loss of synteny blocks. Genome architecture, including repeat content and potential whole-genome duplication signatures, will be assessed using RepeatMasker and custom scripts, building on Boore's expertise in repeat-mediated recombination.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, comparative genomic framework for the obligate biotrophy transition, identifying core, convergent genetic losses versus lineage-specific adaptations. It will generate specific, testable predictions about essential pathogen genes that are dispensable in obligate lineages, informing future functional studies. The analysis will also contribute methodologically by applying tools from mitochondrial and whole-genome duplication research to questions of pathogen genome reduction.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies noted gene loss in individual obligate pathogens, this proposal systematically tests for convergence across multiple independent evolutionary transitions using contemporary comparative genomics methods. It uniquely leverages expertise in genome architecture evolution, developed from studying snail mitochondrial and nuclear genomes, to investigate structural genome changes accompanying this major life-history shift in pathogens.

proposals.title: Dating Gene Loss Events in Oomycete Mitochondrial Genomes Using Phylogenomic Methods from Bacterial Genome Reduction Studies
proposals.motivation: Oomycetes, such as Phytophthora species, are devastating plant pathogens with highly reduced and rapidly evolving mitochondrial genomes, characterized by extensive gene loss and non-standard genome architecture. Pre-2026, the timing and evolutionary drivers of these gene losses remain poorly resolved, limiting our understanding of mitochondrial genome streamlining in non-animal lineages. Methods developed to date gene losses in highly reduced bacterial endosymbiont genomes offer a powerful, yet untapped, framework for analyzing similar processes in eukaryotic organelles.
proposals.hypothesis: Phylogenetic reconciliation and probabilistic models of gene loss, adapted from bacterial genome evolution, can be applied to oomycete mitochondrial genomes to accurately date key gene loss events and correlate them with major shifts in oomycete ecology and pathogenicity.
proposals.method: We will compile a high-quality dataset of oomycete mitochondrial genomes, leveraging long-read sequencing data (e.g., PacBio) to resolve complex architectures as in Boore's prior work on snails. Using a robust, time-calibrated species phylogeny, we will apply phylogenetic reconciliation algorithms (e.g., ALE, GeneRax) developed for modeling gene family evolution in bacteria to infer the most parsimonious history of mitochondrial gene losses. Bayesian molecular clock approaches will then be used to estimate divergence times and place loss events on the timeline of oomycete evolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first temporal framework for mitochondrial gene loss in oomycetes, linking genomic simplification to key adaptive radiations. It will establish a novel methodological bridge between bacterial and eukaryotic organelle genomics, offering a new toolkit for studying genome reduction across the tree of life.
proposals.novelty: While gene loss is well-documented, applying formal phylogenetic dating methods from bacterial genome reduction to date specific losses in eukaryotic organelles is a novel synthesis. This approach moves beyond cataloging losses to testing explicit hypotheses about the tempo and drivers of organelle genome evolution.

proposals.title: Beyond Gene Loss: The Role of Structural Variation, Regulatory Innovation, and Mitochondrial Genome Dynamics in Chordate Genome Evolution
proposals.motivation: A dominant narrative in chordate genome evolution focuses on gene loss and simplification as a primary driver of morphological and physiological change. However, recent work in diverse invertebrate systems, such as the New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum, reveals that complex genomic changes—including whole-genome duplications, introgression, and mitochondrial genome restructuring—are critical evolutionary forces. This proposal challenges the gene-centric simplification model by investigating whether structural and regulatory innovations, rather than mere gene number reduction, underpin key chordate adaptations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that major transitions in chordate evolution are driven not primarily by gene loss, but by structural genomic rearrangements, the evolution of novel cis-regulatory elements, and changes in mitochondrial genome architecture and function.
proposals.method: We will employ a comparative genomics approach across key chordate lineages (e.g., tunicates, lancelets, vertebrates). Using long-read sequencing (PacBio/Oxford Nanopore) and Hi-C scaffolding, we will generate high-quality, chromosome-level assemblies to detect structural variants and synteny breaks. We will predict conserved non-coding elements (CNEs) and cis-regulatory modules using phyloP and machine learning models (e.g., CNEr, DeepSEA). Concurrently, we will sequence and assemble complete mitochondrial genomes using single-molecule methods to analyze architecture, repeat-mediated recombination, and potential chloroplast-like features, as seen in some invertebrates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more nuanced, systems-level view of chordate genome evolution, shifting the focus from a tally of gene losses to the integrative role of genome structure and regulation. It will deliver a high-quality genomic resource for understudied chordates and establish a methodological framework linking nuclear and mitochondrial genome evolution.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the prevailing 'loss-centric' paradigm in chordate genomics by explicitly testing the contribution of structural and regulatory innovation, an approach informed by insights from complex invertebrate genome evolution but not yet systematically applied to chordates.

proposals.title: Functional and Evolutionary Analysis of Lineage-Specific Gene Innovations in the Mitochondrial and Nuclear Genomes of Potamopyrgus antipodarum
proposals.motivation: The New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum is a premier model for studying the maintenance of sexual reproduction and genome evolution, with recent work revealing a whole-genome duplication and complex mitochondrial genome architecture. However, the functional roles of lineage-specific genes that arose from these genomic events, particularly those potentially involved in metabolic innovation or reproductive mode, remain unknown. Testing their function is crucial for linking genomic novelty to the organism's unique biology and ecological success.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that lineage-specific genes in the P. antipodarum nuclear and mitochondrial genomes, including those potentially derived from the whole-genome duplication or lateral gene transfer, encode functions critical for its adaptation to diverse habitats and its reproductive plasticity.
proposals.method: We will first identify candidate lineage-specific genes through comparative genomics using pre-2026 tools (BLAST, OrthoFinder) against a database of molluscan and metazoan genomes. For functional testing, we will use RNA interference (RNAi) or CRISPR-Cas9 (if feasible in this system, based on pre-2026 molluscan protocols) to knock down/out candidate genes in sexual and asexual lineages. Phenotypic effects on key traits (e.g., fecundity, shell integrity, stress response, mitochondrial respiration) will be quantified. We will also analyze gene expression (RNA-seq) across reproductive modes and environments to correlate specific innovations with ecological context.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly link specific genomic innovations to phenotypic function in a key evolutionary model, moving beyond sequence description. It will provide mechanistic insight into how genome duplication and mitochondrial recombination contribute to adaptive potential and the maintenance of sexual reproduction.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on P. antipodarum has described its genome structure, this proposal uniquely integrates functional genomics to test the adaptive value of lineage-specific genes, bridging the gap between genome evolution and organismal phenotype in a system with known sexual/asexual dynamics.

proposals.title: Synteny Mapping Reveals the Architecture of Chordate Genome Evolution: A Plant Genomics Approach Applied to Vertebrate and Invertebrate Lineages
proposals.motivation: Understanding large-scale genome evolution in chordates, including events like whole-genome duplications (WGDs) and chromosomal rearrangements, is fundamental to explaining morphological innovation and diversity. Pre-2026, high-resolution synteny analysis tools (e.g., MCScanX, DAGchainer, SynMap) have been powerfully applied in plant genomics to dissect complex polyploid histories, but their use in animal systems, particularly for comparing across deep chordate divergences, has been limited. This represents a significant methodological gap, as these tools could resolve ancestral genomic architectures and the fate of duplicated genes with unprecedented clarity.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying plant-derived synteny mapping algorithms to chordate genomes will reveal previously unresolved patterns of conserved synteny, micro-synteny decay, and the genomic footprint of ancient whole-genome duplications, particularly the 2R events in early vertebrates. We hypothesize that this approach will identify lineage-specific structural evolution linked to key chordate traits.
proposals.method: We will apply established plant synteny tools (MCScanX for collinearity, SynMap for whole-genome alignment visualization, and DAGchainer for identifying homologous chains) to high-quality genome assemblies from key chordate lineages, including cephalochordates (Branchiostoma), cyclostomes (lamprey), teleost fish, and tetrapods. The analysis pipeline will involve pairwise and multiple genome comparisons to reconstruct ancestral blocks, using the well-annotated plant genomes (e.g., Arabidopsis, Brassica) as a methodological benchmark for detecting WGD signatures. We will integrate this with gene ontology enrichment analysis of conserved syntenic blocks to link structure to function.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, high-resolution map of syntenic conservation and rearrangement across chordate evolution, directly testing hypotheses about vertebrate genome duplication events. It will establish a robust analytical framework for cross-lineage animal genome comparison, bridging plant and animal genomics methodologies. The results will offer new insights into how genomic architecture constrains or facilitates the evolution of gene families involved in development and reproduction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct application of sophisticated plant genomics synteny toolkits, developed for complex polyploid systems, to the long-standing questions of chordate genome evolution. This cross-disciplinary methodological transfer is non-obvious, as these tools are rarely employed in animal evolutionary genomics despite their proven power to dissect deep duplicative history.

proposals.title: Population Genomics of Ciona intestinalis: Leveraging Whole-Genome Duplication and Mitochondrial Architecture to Understand the Evolution of a Key Chordate Model
proposals.motivation: The solitary ascidian Ciona intestinalis is a foundational model for chordate development and genomics, yet its population-level genomic diversity is poorly characterized. Pre-2026 work on Potamopyrgus snails demonstrated how whole-genome duplication (WGD) and mitochondrial genome architecture shape evolutionary trajectories in non-model invertebrates. Applying this comparative framework to Ciona, which also possesses a WGD event in its lineage and a highly recombinant mitochondrial genome, will reveal how these genomic features interact with population processes to influence chordate genome evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the ancestral WGD event in Ciona has provided a reservoir of genetic variation upon which population-level processes act, and that its unique, recombinant mitochondrial genome architecture shows signatures of selection and demographic history distinct from the nuclear genome.
proposals.method: We will generate high-coverage, long-read (PacBio HiFi/ONT) genome assemblies for 20-30 Ciona individuals from globally distributed populations, building on the existing draft reference. Population genomic analyses will use ANGSD for variant calling from resequencing data and PSMC for demographic inference. We will specifically annotate and analyze retained WGD-derived paralogs using SynMap and assess mitochondrial genome structural variation via de novo assembly and repeat analysis, mirroring methods from our Potamopyrgus mitochondrial studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution population genomic resource for Ciona, directly testing the evolutionary consequences of WGD in a natural chordate population. It will disentangle the co-evolution of nuclear and mitochondrial genomes, offering insights into the maintenance of genetic diversity and the genomic basis of local adaptation in a key model species.
proposals.novelty: While Ciona's genome is sequenced, no study has integrated population-scale nuclear genome analysis with detailed investigation of its atypical mitochondrial genome, nor explicitly leveraged its WGD history as a comparative framework informed by prior work on invertebrate systems like Potamopyrgus.

proposals.title: Syntenic Anchors and Functional Shifts: A Dual-Locus Phylogenetic Analysis of Female-Biased Gustatory Receptor Evolution in Gastropods
proposals.motivation: The evolutionary mechanisms driving functional expansion in chemosensory genes, particularly the shift of female-biased gustatory receptors (Grs) from purely reproductive roles to broader behaviors like feeding, remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 studies in model systems like *Drosophila* have mapped Gr expression and function, but lack a robust phylogenetic framework linking genomic architecture to these phenotypic transitions. This gap limits our ability to distinguish if functional innovation is driven by sequence evolution alone or is facilitated by local genomic rearrangement.
proposals.hypothesis: Major functional shifts in female-biased Gr genes (e.g., from oviposition to feeding) are correlated with detectable, lineage-specific micro-rearrangements in their genomic neighborhoods, and phylogenetic trees built from conserved syntenic blocks will provide a stable backbone against which the tempo and mode of Gr sequence evolution and functional change can be reliably traced.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing whole-genome assemblies (e.g., from *Potamopyrgus antipodarum* and related gastropods) and generate new long-read data for key taxa to precisely define Gr loci and their flanking syntenic blocks. Phylogenies will be constructed using (1) Gr coding sequences via maximum likelihood (RAxML/IQ-TREE) and (2) conserved gene order and orientation data from flanking regions using rearrangement distance algorithms (e.g., implemented in DRIMM-Synteny or i-ADHoRe). Incongruence between the sequence-based and synteny-based trees will be tested statistically (e.g., using the Approximately Unbiased test in IQ-TREE) to identify lineages associated with putative functional shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, dual-locus phylogenetic framework that integrates sequence evolution with genomic context to study gene family functional diversification. It will provide concrete evidence for or against the role of local genomic instability in facilitating major behavioral adaptations in chemoreception, moving beyond correlative expression studies to a causal, evolutionary genomic understanding.
proposals.novelty: While Gr phylogenetics and synteny analyses exist separately, their synergistic use to trace the history of specific functional shifts is unprecedented. Applying this integrated approach to the *Potamopyrgus* system, with its well-characterized sexual/asexual lineages and recent whole-genome duplication, offers a unique opportunity to control for genomic background while testing our core hypothesis.

proposals.title: A Neutral Framework for Gene Duplication: Testing the Null Hypothesis of Non-Adaptive Evolution in the Potamopyrgus System
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in genomics assumes gene duplications are primarily a source of evolutionary novelty and adaptation. This adaptationist bias mirrors historical views in paleontology, which were challenged by neutral theory and fossil record analyses demonstrating widespread stasis. A rigorous, population-genetic framework is needed to test whether most duplications are neutral or slightly deleterious, separating rare adaptive events from genomic background noise.
proposals.hypothesis: The majority of retained gene duplicates, including those from whole-genome duplication events, evolve under neutral or slightly deleterious regimes, with positive selection being the exception rather than the rule.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Potamopyrgus antipodarum system, with its recent whole-genome duplication and coexisting sexual/asexual lineages, as a natural laboratory. Using high-coverage population genomic data from multiple lineages, we will apply site frequency spectrum and divergence-based tests (e.g., McDonald-Kreitman extensions for duplicates) to estimate the distribution of fitness effects (DFE) for duplicated genes. We will compare patterns between sexual and asexual populations to control for demographic history and recombination effects, and integrate analyses of mitochondrial and chloroplast-like repeat architectures to assess genomic context.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, testable null model for gene duplication evolution, shifting the burden of proof for adaptive claims. It will deliver a concrete methodological toolkit for applying neutral theory to genomic data and yield specific insights into the evolutionary fate of duplicates following the well-characterized whole-genome duplication in P. antipodarum.
proposals.novelty: It directly imports the conceptual shift from macroevolution (challenging adaptationism with neutral theory) into genomics, applying it to gene duplication—a process typically viewed through an adaptationist lens. The explicit use of sexual/asexual P. antipodarum lineages provides a unique control for testing neutral expectations.

proposals.title: Comparative Genomic Analysis of Gustatory Receptor (Gr) Gene Family Evolution Across Lepidoptera
proposals.motivation: The gustatory receptor (Gr) gene family is crucial for chemosensation, feeding, and host-plant selection in Lepidoptera, directly impacting ecological adaptation and speciation. While Gr evolution has been studied in a few model species, a comprehensive comparative analysis across a broad phylogenetic range of butterflies and moths is lacking. Such an analysis is essential to understand how gene family expansion, contraction, and sequence evolution correlate with dietary shifts and life-history traits in this ecologically vital insect order.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the evolutionary dynamics of the Gr gene family—including lineage-specific expansions, gene losses, and signatures of positive selection—are strongly correlated with major dietary transitions (e.g., from polyphagy to oligophagy) across the Lepidoptera phylogeny.
proposals.method: We will perform a comparative genomic analysis using publicly available, high-quality lepidopteran genomes (pre-2026) from repositories like NCBI. Gr genes will be identified in each genome using a combination of BLASTp and HMMER searches with known insect Gr protein domains. Orthologous groups will be delineated using OrthoFinder. Phylogenetic reconciliation using NOTUNG will be employed to infer gene duplication and loss events. Tests for positive selection will be conducted on specific branches and clades using site and branch-site models in PAML.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, phylogenetically informed map of Gr gene family evolution in Lepidoptera, linking genomic changes to ecological phenotypes. It will identify candidate Gr subfamilies associated with key dietary adaptations, offering a functional genomic resource for future studies on insect-plant interactions and the evolution of chemosensation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in scaling a detailed gene family evolution analysis—akin to methods used in Boore's prior work on snails—to a broad, ecologically diverse taxonomic group (Lepidoptera) to explicitly test macroevolutionary hypotheses about gene family dynamics and adaptation, rather than focusing on a single model system.

proposals.title: Experimental Validation of Gustatory and Olfactory Receptor Gene Models in the New Zealand Mud Snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum
proposals.motivation: The recent whole-genome sequence of Potamopyrgus antipodarum, a key model for studying the maintenance of sexual reproduction, has revealed a complex genomic architecture including a whole-genome duplication. In silico gene predictions, particularly for chemosensory gene families like gustatory (Gr) and olfactory (Or) receptors, are crucial for understanding sensory ecology and behavior, but these models remain computationally derived and unverified. Experimental confirmation is essential for accurate downstream functional and evolutionary analyses, especially in this non-model gastropod system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the predicted Gr and Or gene models in the P. antipodarum genome assembly are largely accurate in their exon-intron structures but will require refinement of 5' and 3' UTRs and potential identification of alternative splice variants through full-length cDNA sequencing.
proposals.method: We will design primers spanning the predicted start and stop codons of selected Gr and Or genes from the published genome. Total RNA will be extracted from sensory-rich tissues (tentacles, osphradium) of sexual and asexual lineages. Reverse transcription will generate cDNA, followed by PCR amplification and cloning into a sequencing vector. Sanger sequencing of multiple clones per gene will yield full-length cDNA sequences. These will be aligned to the genomic scaffold using tools like BLAST and GMAP to validate exon boundaries and annotate UTRs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimentally validated set of chemosensory gene models for P. antipodarum, creating a reliable foundation for studies on gene family evolution post-whole-genome duplication and sensory-driven ecological adaptation. It will also produce a benchmark dataset to improve the accuracy of in silico gene prediction pipelines for other gastropod genomes.
proposals.novelty: While genomic resources exist for P. antipodarum, this study moves beyond computational prediction to provide essential experimental validation of key sensory gene families, integrating directly with the unique context of its mixed reproductive modes and recent whole-genome duplication.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Profiling of Gustatory and Olfactory Receptor Expression Across Larval Development and Adult Sensory Tissues in Potamopyrgus antipodarum
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on the New Zealand mud snail, Potamopyrgus antipodarum, has established it as a key model for studying genome evolution, whole-genome duplication, and the maintenance of sexual reproduction. However, the functional genomic basis of its sensory biology, particularly chemoreception, remains unexplored. Characterizing the expression of gustatory and olfactory receptors (Gr/Or) across life stages and diverse tissues is crucial for linking its remarkable genomic plasticity to ecological adaptation and behavioral phenotypes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Gr/Or gene families, expanded through the recent whole-genome duplication, exhibit stage- and tissue-specific expression patterns, reflecting distinct chemosensory demands in larval dispersal, settlement, and adult foraging. Specifically, larval stages will express a unique subset of receptors critical for habitat selection.
proposals.method: We will generate strand-specific RNA-seq libraries from larval stages (veliger, pediveliger) and adult tissues (osphradium, mantle edge, foot) of sexual and asexual P. antipodarum lineages. Reads will be aligned to the reference genome using HISAT2, and transcript abundance will be quantified with StringTie. Differential expression analysis of Gr/Or gene families will be performed using DESeq2, with clustering of expression profiles across stages/tissues conducted via WGCNA to identify co-regulated modules. Validation will use qPCR on a subset of candidate receptors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive map of chemosensory gene expression in a gastropod model for genome evolution, directly linking genomic duplication events to phenotypic potential. It will establish a functional foundation for studying how sensory systems evolve in mixed sexual/asexual systems and may identify larval-stage-specific receptors as targets for understanding dispersal and settlement mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While prior work sequenced the genome and mitochondrion, no study has functionally profiled expanded gene families like Gr/Or across development in P. antipodarum. Extending analysis beyond adult appendages to larval stages and understudied sensory organs (e.g., osphradium) offers a novel life-history and anatomical perspective on chemoreceptor evolution.

proposals.title: Testing the Macroevolutionary Role of Glutathione S-Transferase (GST) Gene Duplication in Driving Host Plant Diversification in Potamopyrgus Snails
proposals.motivation: The New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum is a premier model for studying the maintenance of sexual reproduction and genome evolution, with recent work revealing a whole-genome duplication (WGD). Glutathione S-transferase (GST) genes are crucial for detoxification and have been implicated in dietary adaptation. It remains untested whether lineage-specific duplication rates of these key metabolic genes are a driver of macroevolutionary diversification, specifically the expansion into novel host plant ecological niches.
proposals.hypothesis: Lineage-specific rates of GST gene family expansion, potentially accelerated by the recent WGD, are positively correlated with and predict increased rates of host plant diversity in Potamopyrgus snails.
proposals.method: We will first annotate and quantify GST gene copy number variation across available high-quality genomes and transcriptomes of sexual and asexual Potamopyrgus lineages. Using a time-calibrated phylogeny, we will apply phylogenetic comparative methods, specifically Binary State Speciation and Extinction (BiSSE) and Multi-State Speciation and Extinction (MuSSE) models in the R package 'diversitree', to test for trait-dependent diversification. The 'trait' will be a discrete or continuous measure of GST duplication rate, while diversification rates will be inferred from the phylogeny and correlated with independently compiled data on host plant range breadth for each lineage.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, phylogenetically robust test of the hypothesis that specific gene family expansions can drive ecological diversification at a macroevolutionary scale. It will link the well-studied genomic architecture of Potamopyrgus with its ecological radiation, offering a mechanistic understanding of how WGD and gene duplication contribute to niche breadth and speciation.
proposals.novelty: While gene duplications are often invoked in adaptation, explicit phylogenetic tests of their role as drivers of lineage diversification are rare. Applying state-of-the-art diversification models to a system with a known, recent WGD and well-characterized reproductive modes allows us to disentangle the effects of genome duplication from other factors on ecological radiation.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic Comparative Methods for Detecting Selection on Copy Number Variable Gene Regions in the Potamopyrgus Model System
proposals.motivation: Copy number variation (CNV) of gene regions (CNV-Grs) is a major source of adaptive genetic diversity, but distinguishing selection from neutral drift in population genomic data remains challenging. Standard tests like Fst or Tajima's D often lack power for complex, multi-allelic CNVs and cannot easily account for shared population history. The New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum, with its mixed sexual/asexual lineages, mitochondrial recombination, and recent whole-genome duplication, provides an ideal, genetically complex system to develop new methods for detecting selection on structural variants.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling CNV-Gr frequency evolution on a population-level phylogeny, we can construct a more accurate neutral null model, thereby increasing power to detect CNV-Grs under recent positive or balancing selection correlated with reproductive mode or environmental gradients.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing whole-genome data from sexual and asexual P. antipodarum lineages and related species to construct a population phylogeny/haplotype network using nuclear SNPs. For each CNV-Gr, we will map its presence/absence or copy number as a continuous trait onto the tips of this tree. We will then fit neutral evolutionary models (e.g., Brownian motion, Ornstein-Uhlenbeck) using PCM frameworks (e.g., `phylolm` in R) to these trait data. Signatures of selection will be identified via likelihood ratio tests comparing neutral models to models incorporating selective regimes (e.g., reproductive mode) or via detection of exceptionally rapid trait evolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, phylogenetically-aware statistical framework for detecting selection on CNVs, moving beyond SNP-centric approaches. Applying it to Potamopyrgus will directly identify candidate CNV-Grs involved in adaptation, particularly those linked to the maintenance of sex, and provide a generalizable method for population genomics of non-model organisms.
proposals.novelty: While PCMs are standard for cross-species traits, their adaptation to model allele frequency evolution of structural variants within a species complex is novel. This explicitly bridges macroevolutionary modeling techniques with population genomics to solve a persistent problem in detecting selection on CNVs.

proposals.title: The Symbiotic Counterpoint: Assessing the Relative Genomic and Symbiotic Contributions to Terrestrialization in Molluscan Lineages
proposals.motivation: The transition to land is a major evolutionary innovation, traditionally attributed to genomic adaptations in key taxa like plants and arthropods. However, the role of microbial symbioses in facilitating this transition, particularly in non-model invertebrates, remains underexplored. This gap limits our understanding of whether terrestrialization is fundamentally a genomic process or one equally dependent on partnerships.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for molluscan lineages with terrestrial representatives, such as gastropods, the genomic signatures of terrestrial adaptation will be less pronounced or distinct in groups where symbiotic relationships with microbes for nutrient acquisition, desiccation tolerance, or detoxification are prevalent.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Potamopyrgus system, where whole-genome resources and mitochondrial architecture are established, as a freshwater/estuarine outgroup. Using long-read (PacBio) and Hi-C sequencing, we will assemble high-quality nuclear and mitochondrial genomes for closely related terrestrial pulmonate snails. Comparative genomics will identify candidate terrestrial adaptation genes. Concurrently, we will use 16S/ITS metabarcoding and metatranscriptomics of snail tissues to characterize conserved, vertically transmitted microbial symbionts and their expressed functions. Phylogenetically-informed comparative analyses will test for correlations between genomic evolutionary rates and symbiont complexity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide one of the first integrated genomic-symbiotic frameworks for understanding terrestrialization in animals. It will generate novel genome resources for terrestrial gastropods and directly test the relative contribution of two major evolutionary mechanisms. The findings will challenge or refine the purely genomic narrative of major evolutionary transitions.
proposals.novelty: While symbioses are known in specific terrestrial invertebrates (e.g., aphids), a systematic, genome-enabled comparison of symbiotic versus genomic contributions across a terrestrialization boundary within a tractable molluscan clade is novel. It applies modern sequencing tools from Boore's recent work to a fundamental eco-evolutionary question.

proposals.title: Functional Genomics of Terrestrial Adaptation: Characterizing Stress-Tolerance Genes in the Invasive Lineage of Potamopyrgus antipodarum
proposals.motivation: The New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum is a powerful model for studying the genomic basis of adaptation, particularly its recent, successful invasion of terrestrial and semi-terrestrial habitats in Europe and North America. Pre-2026 genomic resources, including a high-quality reference genome and identified introgression events, provide an unprecedented opportunity to pinpoint genes responsible for tolerating temperature and desiccation stress, key factors in range expansion and climate change resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the invasive, asexual lineage of P. antipodarum possesses a unique suite of genes, acquired through introgression or retained from a whole-genome duplication, that confer enhanced tolerance to thermal and hydric stress, and that these genes show signatures of positive selection.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing whole-genome sequence data from sexual and asexual lineages, including the recently sequenced P. estuarinus and P. kaitunuparaoa, to perform comparative genomics and identify candidate genes under selection in terrestrial populations. Candidate genes will be functionally characterized using RNA interference (RNAi) knockdown in live snails, followed by controlled stress assays measuring survival, water loss, and heat shock protein expression under simulated terrestrial conditions. Gene expression profiles will be validated via qPCR and in situ hybridization.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly link specific genomic regions to a critical ecological phenotype—terrestrial stress tolerance—in a renowned model for sex and invasion biology. It will provide one of the first functional validations of adaptive introgression in mollusks and elucidate the genetic architecture underlying a major habitat transition.
proposals.novelty: While previous work identified introgression and whole-genome duplication in this system, this proposal moves beyond correlation to direct functional testing of candidate genes for a key adaptive trait, integrating comparative genomics with physiological validation in a non-model organism.

proposals.title: Reconstructing Ancestral Streptophyte Genomes to Decipher Genomic Innovations at the Water-to-Land Transition
proposals.motivation: The colonization of land by streptophyte algae was a pivotal evolutionary transition, but the genomic architecture of the ancestral terrestrial plant remains poorly understood. Pre-2026 studies on genome evolution in models like Potamopyrgus snails demonstrate how whole-genome duplications and mitochondrial dynamics shape adaptation, yet these computational paleogenomic methods have not been systematically applied to the key plant lineage transition. Inferring ancestral genomes at this node would reveal the core genomic toolkit that enabled survival in a desiccating environment.
proposals.hypothesis: The ancestral genome of the first land plants was characterized by specific, retained whole-genome duplications and a reconfigured organellar genome architecture that provided genetic raw material for stress tolerance and developmental novelty. We hypothesize that signatures of these events will be detectable in the nuclear and mitochondrial genomes of extant charophyte algae and early-diverging land plants.
proposals.method: We will apply a computational paleogenomics pipeline to high-quality genomes from streptophyte algae (e.g., Klebsormidium, Chara) and bryophytes (e.g., Marchantia, Physcomitrella). Using ANGES for ancestral genome reconstruction and Dollo parsimony models, we will infer gene content and order. For mitochondrial genomes, we will use a chloroplast-like repeat analysis, inspired by findings in Potamopyrgus, to model recombination dynamics in ancestors. The pipeline will integrate CAFE for gene family evolution and Notung for phylogenomic reconciliation to date duplication events.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first inferred ancestral genome architectures for the plant water-to-land transition, identifying core gene families and structural variants associated with terrestrialization. It will directly test the role of whole-genome duplication prior to land colonization and provide a genomic framework for understanding the evolution of stress response and developmental pathways.
proposals.novelty: While paleogenomics has been used in animals and some plants, a targeted reconstruction focusing on the critical streptophyte node, integrating both nuclear and organellar genome dynamics informed by non-model system insights (e.g., Potamopyrgus), is novel. The approach explicitly links methods from asexual/sexual genome evolution studies to a major evolutionary transition in plants.

proposals.title: Comparative Pan-Genomics of Bryophytes Reveals Genomic Architecture of Terrestrial Adaptation and Asexual Reproduction
proposals.motivation: Bryophytes (mosses, liverworts, hornworts) represent the earliest diverging lineages of land plants and hold key genomic signatures of terrestrial adaptation. However, understanding of their genomic diversity is limited to a few reference genomes, obscuring the full gene repertoire and structural variation critical for survival in non-aquatic environments. A comparative pan-genomic approach across diverse bryophyte species will elucidate the core and accessory genomes associated with fundamental land plant traits and reproductive strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that bryophyte pan-genomes will exhibit extensive accessory genomes enriched for genes involved in stress response and desiccation tolerance, and that clonal, asexual lineages (analogous to *Potamopyrgus antipodarum*) will show distinct patterns of structural variation and genome stability compared to sexual relatives.
proposals.method: We will generate high-quality, chromosome-scale genome assemblies for 10-15 phylogenetically diverse bryophyte species using PacBio HiFi or Oxford Nanopore long-read sequencing, complemented by Hi-C scaffolding. Pan-genomes will be constructed for major clades using the iterative assembly and graph-based methods implemented in tools like minigraph or pggb. We will annotate genes using BRAKER2, identify core and accessory genomic regions, and specifically analyze mitochondrial genome architecture and recombination patterns, building on Boore's expertise in non-standard mitochondrial genomics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comparative pan-genomic resource for bryophytes, identifying candidate genes and genomic structures underpinning the colonization of land. It will provide a novel genomic perspective on the evolution of asexual reproduction in plants, bridging concepts from invertebrate model systems like *P. antipodarum* to early plant evolution.
proposals.novelty: While pan-genomics is established in crops and some animal models, its application to resolve deep evolutionary questions in non-model, early-diverging plants is novel. Integrating nuclear and organellar genome analysis in a pan-genomic framework to study both adaptation and reproductive mode is an unexplored synthesis.

proposals.title: Gene Family Simplification Following Terrestrial Colonization: A Comparative Genomic Analysis Using Potamopyrgus Snails as a Model
proposals.motivation: The transition from aquatic to terrestrial life imposes profound physiological and environmental challenges, yet the genomic consequences, particularly the streamlining of gene families, remain poorly characterized. Pre-2026 studies in model systems like Potamopyrgus antipodarum, which exhibits both sexual and asexual lineages and has experienced a recent whole-genome duplication, provide a unique foundation. This system allows us to disentangle the effects of colonization history from reproductive mode on gene family evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that terrestrial colonization in gastropods is associated with significant simplification (i.e., contraction and loss) in specific gene families related to osmoregulation, sensory perception, and stress response, and that this pattern is distinct from gene family dynamics driven by asexuality or whole-genome duplication.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing whole-genome assemblies for P. antipodarum, P. estuarinus, and P. kaitunuparaoa, alongside new long-read sequencing (PacBio HiFi) for key terrestrial and aquatic lineages. Orthologous gene families will be identified using OrthoFinder v2.5.4. Family size trajectories will be reconstructed across a calibrated phylogeny using CAFE 5. Enrichment analysis of significantly simplified families in terrestrial lineages will be performed using g:Profiler, focusing on Gene Ontology terms. Analyses will control for the known whole-genome duplication and compare sexual vs. asexual clades.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify a core set of gene families consistently simplified during land colonization, providing a genomic signature of this major evolutionary transition. It will clarify whether asexuality, a common feature in invasive Potamopyrgus, accelerates or alters this simplification process. The findings will contribute a novel genomic perspective to the study of adaptation to extreme environmental shifts.
proposals.novelty: While genome duplication and asexuality have been studied in Potamopyrgus, a targeted investigation of gene family simplification as an adaptive response to terrestrial life is novel. The proposal uniquely integrates the system's known genomic features (duplication, reproductive polymorphism) to isolate the specific signal of habitat transition.

proposals.title: Resolving the Origin of Shared Mitochondrial Gene Order in Potamopyrgus: A Single Translocation versus Convergent Evolution
proposals.motivation: The discovery of a chloroplast-like mitochondrial genome architecture in Potamopyrgus antipodarum, characterized by large inverted repeats and repeat-mediated recombination, raises fundamental questions about the evolution of mitochondrial gene order.
