Shared gene arrangements between lineages could arise from a single, shared evolutionary event or through convergent evolution, with profound implications for understanding mitochondrial genome stability, recombination, and the utility of gene order in phylogenetics.
proposals.hypothesis: The shared, derived mitochondrial gene order observed among Potamopyrgus species is the result of a single, unique translocation event in their common ancestor, rather than convergent evolution or multiple independent rearrangements.
proposals.method: We will leverage available whole-genome sequences for sexual and asexual P. antipodarum lineages, P. estuarinus, and P. kaitunuparaoa. Using comparative genomics, we will precisely map the breakpoints of the gene order rearrangement. We will then employ phylogenetic analysis of the flanking sequences and the rearranged block using maximum likelihood methods (e.g., RAxML) to test for monophyly. A coalescent-based analysis of sequence divergence times around the breakpoints will determine if they share a common temporal origin.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively test a core assumption in mitochondrial genomics—that shared gene order implies shared ancestry—within a novel model system for genome evolution. It will provide a concrete evolutionary history for a major mitochondrial rearrangement and clarify the mechanisms (single event vs. recombination-prone regions) governing metazoan mitochondrial genome architecture.
proposals.novelty: While mitochondrial gene orders are compared, explicit tests to distinguish single events from convergence at the sequence level are rare. Applying this rigorous phylogenetic approach to the unique, recombination-active mitochondrial system of Potamopyrgus, a model for sexual reproduction, is novel.

proposals.title: Ancestral or Novel? Testing the Evolutionary Origin of a Unique Mitochondrial Gene Arrangement in Potamopyrgus Using Deep Sequencing of Arthropod Outgroups
proposals.motivation: The discovery of a highly rearranged, chloroplast-like mitochondrial genome in the New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum, with evidence of repeat-mediated recombination, challenges our understanding of mitochondrial genome stability in animals. Determining whether such an arrangement is a lineage-specific novelty or an ancestral state retained from an unsequenced arthropod lineage is crucial for reconstructing deep evolutionary history and understanding the mechanisms of mitochondrial genome evolution. This question remains unresolved due to sparse sampling of key outgroup taxa.
proposals.hypothesis: The unique mitochondrial gene order in P. antipodarum is not an ancestral feature but a derived, lineage-specific rearrangement that arose after its divergence from related gastropod and arthropod lineages.
proposals.method: We will employ a targeted sequencing approach using long-read (PacBio or Nanopore) and Illumina platforms to assemble complete mitochondrial genomes from a phylogenetically diverse set of outgroups, focusing on under-sampled arthropod classes (e.g., Pycnogonida, Remipedia, Cephalocarida) and early-diverging gastropods. Gene orders will be systematically compared using genome rearrangement algorithms (e.g., GRAPPA, MGR) and phylogenetic analysis of concatenated protein-coding genes under maximum likelihood. Ancestral gene order will be reconstructed using parsimony and probabilistic methods (e.g., in MLGO or DeClone) on a robust species tree.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively test the ancestry of a highly unusual mitochondrial architecture, providing a clear evolutionary context for a major model system in sexual reproduction research. It will significantly expand the genomic resources for basal arthropod lineages and refine our models of mitochondrial genome structural evolution across Metazoa.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond descriptive reporting of a novel genome structure to a rigorous, phylogenetically grounded test of its origin, leveraging cutting-edge sequencing to target specific taxonomic gaps identified as critical by pre-2026 work. It directly integrates Boore's expertise in mitochondrial genomics and genome rearrangement analysis with his recent focus on Potamopyrgus evolution.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic Survey of Mitochondrial Gene Translocation Events Across Major Arthropod Lineages
proposals.motivation: The discovery of a mitochondrial gene translocation to the nuclear genome in the New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum, facilitated by chloroplast-like repeat-mediated recombination, challenges the paradigm of strict maternal inheritance and stability of animal mitochondrial genomes. Understanding the phylogenetic distribution of such events is crucial for assessing their evolutionary frequency, mechanisms, and potential role in genome evolution, particularly in non-model arthropod groups where such surveys are lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: Mitochondrial gene translocation events, similar to that characterized in P. antipodarum, are not an isolated anomaly but have occurred independently in other arthropod lineages, particularly in myriapods and chelicerates, and may be associated with specific genomic architectures like repetitive elements.
proposals.method: We will generate long-read (PacBio or Oxford Nanopore) whole-genome sequencing data for key species across Myriapoda (centipedes, millipedes) and Chelicerata (spiders, mites, horseshoe crabs). Using a bioinformatics pipeline centered on BLASTn and minimap2, we will screen nuclear assemblies for sequences with high identity to a curated set of arthropod mitochondrial protein-coding and rRNA genes. Putative nuclear mitochondrial DNA segments (NUMTs) will be validated via PCR and Sanger sequencing across genomic and cDNA templates to distinguish transcribed pseudogenes from functional nuclear copies, and their flanking regions will be analyzed for repetitive elements using RepeatMasker.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic assessment of mitochondrial gene translocation prevalence in understudied arthropod subphyla, directly testing the generality of the P. antipodarum finding. It will generate high-quality genomic resources for non-model species and elucidate potential mechanistic links between genome architecture (e.g., repeats, whole-genome duplications) and organelle gene transfer.
proposals.novelty: While NUMTs are known, the targeted search for recent, potentially functional translocations inspired by a specific, well-characterized non-insect model is novel. Extending this search beyond mollusks and insects into myriapods and chelicerates tests whether this is a widespread arthropod phenomenon.

proposals.title: Phylogenomic Dating of Mitochondrial Gene Translocation in Pancrustacea Using Long-Read Sequencing and Fossil-Calibrated Trees
proposals.motivation: The translocation of mitochondrial genes to the nuclear genome is a major evolutionary event with profound implications for genome architecture and function. In Pancrustacea (crustaceans and insects), the translocation of genes like *cox1* is a key genomic trait, but its precise evolutionary timing remains unresolved, hindering our understanding of its adaptive significance and correlation with major cladogenic events. Pinpointing this timing requires a robust, genome-scale phylogenetic framework that has been lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the mitochondrial gene translocation event occurred in a specific, narrow window of time within the Pancrustacea phylogeny, likely associated with a major radiation or a shift in genomic evolutionary rates. This timing can be constrained by integrating high-quality genome assemblies across key lineages with fossil-calibrated molecular dating.
proposals.method: We will generate high-quality, chromosome-level genome assemblies for strategically selected sexual and asexual Potamopyrgus species and other key pancrustacean taxa using PacBio HiFi or Oxford Nanopore long-read sequencing. We will use BLAST and custom bioinformatic pipelines to identify and characterize nuclear-mitochondrial DNA segments (NUMTs) and fully translocated genes. A phylogenomic dataset of conserved single-copy nuclear genes will be assembled using tools like OrthoFinder and aligned with MAFFT. Divergence times will be estimated using Bayesian molecular clock methods (e.g., MCMCTree in PAML or BEAST2), incorporating carefully selected fossil calibrations to date the node preceding the translocation event's fixation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first precise, fossil-calibrated estimate for the timing of mitochondrial gene translocation in Pancrustacea, directly linking a major genomic change to the group's evolutionary timescale. It will establish a robust genomic and methodological framework for studying NUMT evolution and test long-standing questions about the potential drivers, such as whole-genome duplication or shifts in reproductive mode, of such translocations.
proposals.novelty: While mitochondrial gene content is well-studied, explicitly dating a translocation event within a robust, genome-scale phylogenetic context is novel. Leveraging long-read sequencing from model systems like *P. antipodarum*—with its characterized sexual/asexual dynamics and whole-genome duplication—to resolve a deep phylogenetic question represents a unique, cross-scale synthesis.

proposals.title: Automated Detection of Split and Intron-Containing Genes in Complex Eukaryotic Genomes: A Pipeline Integrating Iterative BLAST and Synteny Analysis
proposals.motivation: Accurate gene annotation in non-model eukaryotes, especially those with high intron density or fragmented assemblies, remains a major bottleneck. This is critical for studying genome evolution in systems like the New Zealand mud snail (Potamopyrgus antipodarum), where recent whole-genome duplication and mitochondrial recombination complicate gene identification. Current BLAST-based pipelines often fail to detect split genes, leading to incomplete functional and comparative genomic analyses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an automated pipeline combining iterative, multi-stage BLAST searches with cross-species synteny conservation analysis will significantly improve the detection of intron-containing and fragmented protein-coding genes in complex eukaryotic genomes.
proposals.method: We will develop a Python-based pipeline that first performs a standard tBLASTn, then iteratively extends hits using gapped BLAST (BLASTx) on flanking genomic regions, guided by splice site prediction algorithms (e.g., GeneMark-ES). Candidate split genes will be validated using synteny analysis, comparing gene order and orientation against closely related reference genomes (e.g., other molluscs). The pipeline will be benchmarked on the well-annotated *Drosophila melanogaster* genome before application to *P. antipodarum* and its relatives.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, open-source tool for the community to recover more complete gene sets from draft genomes. Applying it to the *Potamopyrgus* system will yield a more accurate catalog of genes, enabling deeper investigation into the genomic consequences of asexuality, ploidy, and mitochondrial recombination in this model.
proposals.novelty: While iterative BLAST exists, its systematic integration with synteny validation for *de novo* split-gene detection in non-model organisms is not standardized. Tailoring and validating this approach specifically for the challenges posed by recently duplicated and recombinant genomes, like those of *Potamopyrgus*, represents a novel application.

proposals.title: Testing the 2R Hypothesis: A Model-Based Assessment of Segmental Duplication as an Alternative Explanation for Vertebrate Genome Structure
proposals.motivation: The two-round (2R) hypothesis posits that two ancestral whole-genome duplications (WGDs) shaped the vertebrate genome, but this remains controversial. Recent work, including our sequencing of Potamopyrgus antipodarum which revealed a recent WGD, provides a modern comparative framework. This proposal directly challenges the 2R paradigm by testing whether the observed genomic patterns in vertebrates could be better explained by an accumulation of segmental duplications, a critical unresolved question in evolutionary genomics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the paralogon structure and gene family patterns in key vertebrate lineages (e.g., teleost fish, which experienced a third WGD) can be statistically modeled and explained more parsimoniously by a history of recurrent segmental duplications rather than two discrete, ancient WGD events.
proposals.method: We will develop a probabilistic, phylogeny-aware model of gene family evolution that explicitly compares the likelihood of observed gene order and synteny data under a 2R scenario versus a segmental duplication model. Using pre-2026 tools like CAFE 5 for gene family analysis and SynNet for synteny network construction, we will analyze high-quality genomes from vertebrates, cephalochordates (e.g., amphioxus), and tunicates. The model will incorporate parameters for segmental duplication rate, size, and loss, simulating genomic architectures over deep time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, model-based test of a foundational hypothesis in vertebrate evolution. It will deliver a new computational framework for distinguishing WGD from segmental duplication signatures in deep evolutionary history. The results will significantly advance our understanding of the mechanisms driving major genomic complexity.
proposals.novelty: While the 2R hypothesis has been debated, a direct, model-based comparison against a quantitative segmental duplication alternative using contemporary genomic data and methods from non-vertebrate WGD models (like P. antipodarum) is novel. This moves beyond descriptive phylogenomics to explicit hypothesis testing with generative models.

proposals.title: Functional Trajectories of Retained Duplicates: Subfunctionalization and Neofunctionalization Following Whole-Genome Duplication in Potamopyrgus antipodarum
proposals.motivation: Whole-genome duplications (WGDs) are major evolutionary events that provide raw genetic material for innovation, but the processes determining the functional fate of retained duplicate genes remain poorly characterized in non-model, ecologically relevant organisms. The New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum, with its recent WGD and coexisting sexual and asexual lineages, offers a powerful system to study these dynamics in real-time, linking genomic architecture to the maintenance of sexual reproduction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genes retained after the recent WGD in P. antipodarum have undergone non-random functional divergence, with patterns of subfunctionalization (partitioning of ancestral functions) and neofunctionalization (acquisition of novel functions) that differ between sexual and asexual lineages, contributing to their ecological success.
proposals.method: We will leverage the existing whole-genome assembly and resequencing data from sexual and asexual P. antipodarum lineages. Using phylogenetic reconciliation (e.g., Notung) and synteny analysis, we will identify retained WGD-derived paralogs. We will then perform comparative analyses of: 1) sequence evolution (dN/dS ratios using PAML) to detect signatures of positive selection; 2) tissue-specific and developmental stage expression patterns (via RNA-seq) to infer subfunctionalization; and 3) co-expression network analysis (WGCNA) to assess potential novel functional modules. Functional enrichment (GO, KEGG) of divergent paralogs will be conducted.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide one of the first detailed, genome-wide characterizations of post-duplication gene fate in a freshwater gastropod, directly testing classic models of duplicate gene retention. It will generate specific, testable links between WGD, functional genomic evolution, and the unique reproductive polymorphism in this model system, offering insights into how genome duplication fuels adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While WGDs are well-studied in plants and fish, this proposal uniquely integrates the genomic aftermath of a recent WGD with a powerful natural experiment—the coexistence of sexual and asexual lineages—to dissect the evolutionary pressures shaping duplicate gene fate in a novel metazoan system.

proposals.title: Phylogenomic Reconciliation of Gene Duplication Histories in the Potamopyrgus Genus: Distinguishing Whole-Genome Duplication from Segmental Duplications
proposals.motivation: The recent discovery of a whole-genome duplication (WGD) in the asexual Potamopyrgus antipodarum provides a unique opportunity to study the genomic consequences of polyploidy in a natural system. However, distinguishing true WGD-derived paralogs from those arising via segmental duplication or introgression, especially in the context of the complex sexual/asexual lineages and recent speciation within the genus, remains a significant challenge with standard comparative genomics. Robustly resolving this duplication history is critical for understanding the role of WGD in the maintenance of sexual reproduction and adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying phylogenomic reconciliation methods to multi-locus datasets from Potamopyrgus species will robustly infer the timing and scope of the putative WGD event, distinguishing it from lineage-specific segmental duplications and introgression, thereby clarifying the genomic architecture underlying the group's evolution.
proposals.method: We will generate and collate nuclear gene alignments from available and newly sequenced genomes/transcriptomes of P. antipodarum, P. estuarinus, and P. kaitunuparaoa, using an outgroup gastropod. We will employ gene tree-species tree reconciliation algorithms (e.g., ALE, GeneRax) within a coalescent-aware framework. These methods will use a well-supported species tree (based on mitochondrial and single-copy nuclear genes) to map gene family trees, explicitly modeling duplication, transfer, and loss (DTL) events to infer the most parsimonious history of each gene family across the genus.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal, phylogenetically rigorous test of the WGD hypothesis in Potamopyrgus, quantifying the proportion of the genome affected. It will clarify the duplication landscape, offering insights into how WGD and subsequent diploidization interact with reproductive mode and speciation. The resulting reconciled gene trees will be a foundational resource for future studies on gene family evolution in this model system.
proposals.novelty: While WGD was identified via k-mer analysis, this study applies state-of-the-art reconciliation models, common in microbial evolution, to a complex metazoan system with mixed reproductive modes, directly testing the genomic duplication history against alternative hypotheses of piecemeal segmental duplication and introgression.

proposals.title: Genome Contraction and Gene Loss Following Whole-Genome Duplication in the Potamopyrgus antipodarum Species Complex
proposals.motivation: Whole-genome duplications (WGDs) are recognized as major evolutionary events that provide raw genetic material for innovation, but the subsequent processes of diploidization and genome contraction are less understood. In Potamopyrgus antipodarum, a recent WGD has been identified, offering a unique, recent model to study the immediate genomic consequences of duplication. Characterizing the patterns, timing, and functional biases of gene loss is crucial for understanding how genomes stabilize and how sexual vs. asexual lineages differentially manage genomic redundancy.
proposals.hypothesis: Following the recent WGD in the P. antipodarum lineage, rapid and non-random gene loss has occurred, with contraction events concentrated in specific functional categories and genomic regions, and the tempo of loss differing between sexual and asexual populations.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing whole-genome sequences for sexual and asexual P. antipodarum, P. estuarinus, and P. kaitunuparaoa. Using the unduplicated genome of a closely related outgroup species, we will identify orthologous gene groups and systematically catalog gene losses in the duplicated lineage. We will employ phylogenomic reconciliation methods (e.g., using NOTUNG) to time loss events relative to speciation and ploidy transitions. Functional enrichment analysis (e.g., GO term analysis) of lost versus retained paralogs will be conducted, and patterns of loss will be compared between reproductive modes using population genomic data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide one of the first detailed empirical maps of gene loss following a very recent WGD in a non-model animal system. It will test theoretical models of diploidization and quantify how reproductive mode influences genome evolution. The results will directly inform the understanding of how genetic redundancy is resolved, a fundamental process in genome evolution.
proposals.novelty: While prior work in this system identified the WGD, this study systematically inverts the focus to the understudied contraction phase. It uniquely integrates the dimension of reproductive mode (sexual vs. asexual) into the analysis of post-duplication genome dynamics, moving beyond cataloging duplications to understanding their evolutionary fate.

proposals.title: Leveraging Synteny and Comparative Genomics to Resolve Mitochondrial Genome Architecture and Annotation in Potamopyrgus Snails
proposals.motivation: Accurate mitochondrial genome annotation is crucial for evolutionary and population genomics, but remains challenging due to structural complexity, gene rearrangements, and repeat-mediated recombination, as highlighted in recent studies of Potamopyrgus. Pre-2026, automated annotation pipelines often produce inconsistent or incomplete gene models, especially in non-model organisms with unusual architectures, hindering reliable comparative analyses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a comparative synteny-based approach, leveraging newly available whole-genome assemblies from sexual and asexual Potamopyrgus lineages, will significantly improve the accuracy and consistency of mitochondrial gene annotations by identifying conserved gene order blocks and revealing lineage-specific structural variants.
proposals.method: We will generate high-quality mitochondrial genome assemblies for key Potamopyrgus species (P. antipodarum, P. estuarinus) using long-read (PacBio) data. Synteny analysis will be performed using the SyRI (Synteny and Rearrangement Identifier) tool to align these assemblies against each other and against curated molluscan mitochondrial references. Conserved syntenic blocks will be used to validate and manually correct gene boundaries from automated annotations (e.g., MITOS2), with particular focus on tRNA gene identification and the boundaries of non-coding repeat regions implicated in recombination.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, high-confidence set of mitochondrial annotations for a key model system in sexual reproduction research. It will establish a robust synteny-based framework for mitochondrial genome curation applicable to other non-model taxa. Furthermore, it will provide clearer genomic signatures of the structural evolution associated with the whole-genome duplication and reproductive modes in Potamopyrgus.
proposals.novelty: While synteny is a standard tool in nuclear genomics, its systematic application to correct and validate mitochondrial genome annotations, particularly in the context of documented chloroplast-like architecture and recombination, is novel. This approach directly addresses the annotation inconsistencies revealed by single-molecule sequencing in this system.

proposals.title: Testing the Adaptive Hypothesis of Plastid Gene Loss: A Comparative Genomic and Phylogenetic Framework in Potamopyrgus
proposals.motivation: Plastid genome evolution is often characterized by gene loss, typically attributed to neutral processes like non-functionalization following transfer to the nucleus. However, the assumption of neutrality for gene content evolution remains largely untested. This gap is significant because if gene losses are adaptive, perhaps by streamlining genomes for replication or altering energy budgets, it would fundamentally reshape our understanding of organelle genome dynamics and the forces constraining their structure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of plastid gene losses in the gastropod genus Potamopyrgus are adaptive events, driven by selection for increased replication efficiency or metabolic optimization, rather than neutral outcomes of functional relocation.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing and newly generated long-read sequencing data (e.g., PacBio, Oxford Nanopore) for multiple sexual and asexual lineages of P. antipodarum and related species to assemble complete, high-quality plastid genomes. Using a robust, time-calibrated phylogeny, we will employ phylogenetic comparative methods (e.g., BiSSE, HiSSE) to test for correlations between gene loss events and key life-history traits (e.g., reproductive mode, habitat). We will complement this with codon-based selection analyses (dN/dS) on retained genes preceding loss events and model the energetic and replication time costs of lost vs. retained gene suites.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide one of the first direct tests of adaptation in plastid gene content evolution, moving beyond descriptive cataloging of losses. It will establish a novel analytical framework for distinguishing neutral from adaptive organelle genome reduction and offer insights into the potential metabolic and replicative trade-offs shaping these genomes.
proposals.novelty: While gene loss is well-documented, explicitly modeling it as a potentially adaptive trait within a rigorous phylogenetic context is novel. Applying this framework to the non-photosynthetic, chloroplast-like plastid genomes of Potamopyrgus, a system with well-characterized sexual/asexual dynamics, offers a unique opportunity to link genome evolution to life-history evolution.

proposals.title: Gene-Specific Substitution Rate Modeling in the Mitochondrial Genomes of Potamopyrgus: Implications for Asexual Lineage Evolution and Phylogenetic Inference
proposals.motivation: Mitochondrial genomes are crucial for phylogenetic and population genetic studies, but standard analyses often apply uniform substitution models across all 13 protein-coding genes, ignoring well-documented functional and structural heterogeneity. This oversimplification can bias evolutionary rate estimates, divergence times, and phylogenetic reconstructions, particularly in non-model systems like the New Zealand mud snail (Potamopyrgus antipodarum), where complex reproductive modes (sexual vs. asexual) and whole-genome duplication events create unique evolutionary dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mitochondrial protein-coding genes in Potamopyrgus exhibit significantly heterogeneous, gene-specific substitution rates, and that modeling this heterogeneity will reveal distinct evolutionary pressures between sexual and asexual lineages and improve the resolution of species relationships within the genus.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing whole-genome and long-read mitochondrial data for multiple Potamopyrgus species and reproductive lineages. For each of the 13 mitochondrial protein-coding genes, we will separately estimate site-specific evolutionary rates using codon models (e.g., M0, M3) in PAML and site-heterogeneous models in PhyloBayes. We will statistically compare per-gene rate distributions using likelihood ratio tests and Bayesian model comparison. Phylogenies will be reconstructed under both concatenated uniform-rate and partitioned gene-specific rate models to assess topological impacts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for gene-specific mitochondrial evolution in Potamopyrgus, directly testing how reproductive mode influences molecular evolution at a fine scale. It will deliver a more accurate phylogenetic hypothesis for the genus and a set of empirically validated, gene-specific substitution models for future population genetic and comparative studies in mollusks.
proposals.novelty: While gene-specific rates are acknowledged, they are rarely modeled explicitly in mitochondrial phylogenomics of non-model invertebrates. This study integrates this modeling with the unique Potamopyrgus system—characterized by coexisting sexual/asexual lineages and documented mitochondrial structural variation—to ask new questions about the drivers of molecular evolution.

proposals.title: Functional and Evolutionary Consequences of Independent Plastid Gene Losses in Angiosperms: A Comparative Genomics and Transcriptomic Approach
proposals.motivation: The plastid genomes of angiosperms have undergone extensive structural evolution, including numerous independent gene and intron losses. While these losses are well-documented phylogenetically, their functional impact on plant physiology and their role in driving lineage-specific adaptation remain poorly understood. Investigating these consequences is crucial for linking genome reduction to phenotypic evolution, especially in the context of energy metabolism and stress response pathways encoded by plastid genes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that independent losses of specific plastid genes in monocots and eudicots are not evolutionarily neutral but have led to compensatory changes in nuclear gene expression and measurable impacts on photosynthetic efficiency or stress tolerance, contributing to lineage-specific adaptations.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing whole-genome and transcriptome data from public repositories (e.g., NCBI, Phytozome) for species representing independent loss events of key genes (e.g., *accD*, *ycf1*, *ycf2*). Using comparative genomic methods, we will map loss events onto a robust phylogeny. We will then perform differential gene expression analysis (using DESeq2/edgeR) on RNA-seq data from matched tissues to identify compensatory upregulation of nuclear-encoded homologs. For key model species (e.g., *Arabidopsis*, *Oryza*), we will analyze published physiological datasets to correlate gene loss with functional traits.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond cataloging plastid genome structure to explicitly test the functional ramifications of gene loss. It will provide a framework for understanding how organelle genome reduction is integrated into cellular networks and may identify specific nuclear genes that have compensated for these losses, revealing key points of cytonuclear co-evolution.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have documented the pattern of plastid gene loss, this proposal uniquely integrates comparative phylogenomics with transcriptomic and phenotypic data to test for functional consequences, directly addressing the long-standing question of whether these losses are drivers or passengers of adaptation.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic Comparative Analysis of Mitochondrial Genome Architecture and Gene Content in Potamopyrgus Snails: Linking Genome Evolution to Reproductive Mode and Habitat
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies of the New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum have established it as a key model for studying the maintenance of sexual reproduction and genome evolution, revealing a recent whole-genome duplication and complex mitochondrial genome architecture. However, the adaptive significance of mitochondrial gene content variation and structural changes across closely related species and lineages with differing reproductive modes (sexual vs. asexual) and ecological habitats (estuarine vs. freshwater) remains poorly understood. Applying macroecological comparative methods can bridge this gap by formally testing for evolutionary correlations between genomic traits and life-history or environmental variables.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that changes in mitochondrial gene content (e.g., gene duplications, losses) and genome architecture (e.g., repeat-mediated recombination structures) in the Potamopyrgus genus are correlated with shifts in reproductive strategy (sexual/asexual) and specific ecological habitats, reflecting adaptive genomic responses to different evolutionary pressures.
proposals.method: We will construct a time-calibrated phylogeny for Potamopyrgus species and lineages using available nuclear genomic data and newly generated data. Using this phylogeny, we will employ phylogenetic generalized least squares (PGLS) and phylogenetic logistic regression models to test for correlations between mitochondrial genomic traits (e.g., presence/absence of duplicated genes, repeat content, genome size) and predictor variables (reproductive mode, salinity tolerance, geographic range). We will use Bayesian methods, such as those implemented in BayesTraits, to reconstruct ancestral states and assess the directionality of trait evolution, controlling for phylogenetic non-independence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, phylogenetically controlled test of links between mitochondrial genome evolution and key ecological/life-history traits in a model system for sex. It will determine if the remarkable mitochondrial genome structures observed in P. antipodarum are general features of the genus and if they are associated with asexuality or specific environmental challenges. The findings will contribute to broader theories on the role of organelle genome evolution in adaptation and reproductive mode transitions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described mitochondrial genome sequences and structures in Potamopyrgus, no study has yet applied formal phylogenetic comparative methods from macroecology to test explicit hypotheses about the drivers of this variation. The integration of robust comparative phylogenetic analysis with detailed genomic and ecological data from this model system is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Predicting Ecological and Morphological Traits from Plastid Gene Repertoire Evolution in the New Zealand Mud Snail Complex
proposals.motivation: The plastid-like architecture of the Potamopyrgus antipodarum mitochondrial genome, revealed by recent single-molecule sequencing, suggests a unique evolutionary trajectory with potential functional consequences. In plants, plastid gene content is linked to photosynthetic mode and ecological niche, but this predictive framework has not been applied to non-photosynthetic, animal systems with analogous organelle genome structures. This project addresses whether organelle gene repertoire can serve as a general predictive tool for organismal traits, moving beyond descriptive genomics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that lineage-specific patterns of gene retention, loss, and rearrangement in the highly structured mitochondrial genomes of the Potamopyrgus species complex contain signatures that are predictive of key ecological (e.g., salinity tolerance, invasiveness) and life-history (e.g., sexual vs. asexual reproduction) traits.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing whole-genome and mitochondrial genome sequences from sexual and asexual lineages of P. antipodarum and related species. Using a comparative genomics pipeline, we will quantify gene presence/absence, order, and copy number variation across the mitochondrial genome. These features will be used as predictors in supervised machine learning models (e.g., Random Forest or Gradient Boosting) trained on associated phenotypic and ecological metadata (e.g., reproductive mode, habitat salinity, shell morphology) to build predictive trait models. Model performance will be validated using cross-validation and independent lineages.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, gene-content-based framework for predicting organismal traits from organelle genomes, extending a botanical concept to a non-model animal system. It will provide new, testable hypotheses about the functional and adaptive significance of the unusual mitochondrial genome architecture in Potamopyrgus, linking molecular evolution directly to ecology.
proposals.novelty: While predicting traits from nuclear gene content is common, applying this 'inverse problem' approach to animal mitochondrial genomes—and specifically to the chloroplast-like, repeat-rich architecture of Potamopyrgus—is unprecedented. The use of this system uniquely allows us to test if organelle genome structure predicts reproductive mode, a key evolutionary trait.

proposals.title: Phylogenomic Resolution of Potamopyrgus Evolutionary History Using a Genome-Wide Set of Single-Copy Nuclear Orthologs
proposals.motivation: Phylogenetic studies of the New Zealand mud snail genus Potamopyrgus, a key model for studying the maintenance of sexual reproduction, have historically relied on mitochondrial genomes or a handful of nuclear markers. These data sources can be confounded by introgression, incomplete lineage sorting, or, in the case of mitochondrial DNA, sex-biased dispersal and recombination. A robust, genome-scale nuclear phylogeny is therefore essential to accurately resolve species relationships and provide a stable framework for testing hypotheses about the evolution of reproductive modes, ploidy, and historical introgression in this complex system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a phylogeny inferred from hundreds of single-copy nuclear orthologs will resolve the contentious relationships within Potamopyrgus with high support, revealing a species tree that is incongruent with mitochondrial histories due to past introgression events and clarifying the phylogenetic positioning of key asexual and sexual lineages.
proposals.method: We will leverage the published whole-genome assemblies for P. antipodarum and related species. Using OrthoFinder v2.5.4, we will identify single-copy orthologous gene families across all taxa. Individual gene alignments will be generated with MAFFT, and gene trees will be inferred using IQ-TREE 2 under best-fit models. The species tree will be reconstructed using both concatenation (IQ-TREE) and coalescent-based methods (ASTRAL-III) to account for incomplete lineage sorting. Introgression will be tested using D-statistics (ABBA-BABA) and PhyloNetworks.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first highly resolved, genome-wide nuclear phylogeny for Potamopyrgus, providing a definitive reference for evolutionary studies. It will directly test for cytonuclear discordance and historical introgression, offering mechanistic insights into the genomic consequences of hybridization and the origins of asexual lineages. The dataset will also serve as a community resource for future population genomic and comparative studies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has sequenced genomes and examined mitochondrial architecture, this study applies a rigorous, scaled-up phylogenomic approach specifically targeting hundreds of single-copy nuclear loci, moving beyond single-organelle or few-gene analyses to tackle the complex evolutionary history of this model genus with appropriate genomic resolution.

proposals.title: Functional Characterization of Horizontally Acquired Photosynthesis Genes in the Mitochondrial Genome of Potamopyrgus antipodarum
proposals.motivation: The discovery of chloroplast-like architecture and repeat-mediated recombination in the mitochondrial genome of the New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum, including the presence of unique stramenopile-like photosynthesis-related genes, challenges fundamental paradigms of eukaryotic genome evolution and organellar function. Pre-2026 phylogenomic analysis suggests these genes were acquired via horizontal gene transfer (HGT), but their functional activity and potential role in the snail's biology, particularly in relation to its status as a model for sexual/asexual reproduction, remain entirely unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the stramenopile-derived photosynthesis genes identified in the P. antipodarum mitochondrial genome are transcriptionally active and encode functional proteins that may confer a novel metabolic capability, potentially linked to the ecological success and evolutionary dynamics of this invasive species.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-omics approach grounded in pre-2026 methods. First, we will use PacBio or Oxford Nanopore single-molecule sequencing (as in Boore's prior work) for full-length cDNA sequencing (Iso-Seq) to confirm transcription and splicing patterns of the candidate genes. We will then use quantitative PCR and RNA in situ hybridization to assess expression levels and tissue localization. Finally, we will express the putative protein products heterologously in a microbial system (e.g., E. coli or yeast) to assay for enzymatic activity related to chlorophyll biosynthesis or light-harvesting complex assembly.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first functional evidence for active photosynthesis-related genes in a metazoan mitochondrial genome, directly testing the biological significance of a profound HGT event. It will advance our understanding of organellar genome plasticity and could reveal a novel endosymbiosis-derived trait influencing the physiology and invasive ecology of a key model species.
proposals.novelty: While HGT into animal genomes is known, functional characterization of putative photosynthesis genes in a multicellular animal's mitochondrion is unprecedented. This proposal uniquely bridges the gap between the descriptive phylogenomics of the initial discovery and experimental validation of gene function in a non-photosynthetic host.

proposals.title: Comparative Genomic Analysis of Gene Duplication, Regulatory Evolution, and Horizontal Transfer in the Adaptation of Potamopyrgus Snails
proposals.motivation: The relative contributions of gene duplication, regulatory sequence evolution, and horizontal gene transfer (HGT) to adaptive evolution remain a central debate in evolutionary genomics. The New Zealand mud snail Potamopyrgus antipodarum, with its mixed sexual/asexual lineages, recent whole-genome duplication (WGD), and documented mitochondrial recombination, provides a powerful, pre-2026 model system to dissect these mechanisms in a natural context of rapid adaptation and speciation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while the recent WGD provides raw material, adaptive divergence among Potamopyrgus species and lineages is primarily driven by the differential retention and regulatory evolution of duplicated genes, supplemented by HGT events that introduce novel metabolic functions.
proposals.method: We will generate high-quality, chromosome-level genome assemblies for P. antipodarum, P. estuarinus, and P. kaitunuparaoa using PacBio HiFi and Hi-C sequencing. Using pre-2026 tools (e.g., OrthoFinder, CAFE5, BUSTED), we will catalogue gene families, date duplication events, and test for positive selection. Regulatory evolution will be assessed via ATAC-seq and histone modification ChIP-seq across tissues and environments. We will screen for HGT using a phylogeny-based pipeline (e.g., DarkHorse) and validate candidates via PCR and FISH.
proposals.contribution: This work will quantify the mechanistic drivers of adaptation in a key model system, directly testing the 'gene duplication-first' paradigm. It will produce a foundational genomic resource for Potamopyrgus and elucidate how WGD, regulatory plasticity, and HGT interact during speciation.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond cataloging WGD to functionally dissect its adaptive consequences in tandem with other mechanisms, leveraging the unique natural history of Potamopyrgus. It integrates comparative genomics, epigenomics, and HGT detection in a single, replicated system, a synthesis not yet performed pre-2026.

ideas.title: Applying Neutral Theory and Fossil Record Analysis to Gene Duplication
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that gene duplication always increases adaptive capacity by drawing an analogy from paleontology and macroevolution. In that field, the default adaptationist paradigm was successfully challenged by neutral theory and analyses of the fossil record, which showed stasis and non-adaptive change are common. The analogous solution was to shift the burden of proof, requiring positive evidence for adaptation rather than assuming it. Adapted to genomics, this idea proposes treating gene duplications as evolutionary events to be scrutinized like fossils in the genomic record. The research would develop and apply population genetic and comparative genomic methods to rigorously test the null hypothesis that a given duplication, or a class of duplications, is neutral or slightly deleterious, thereby separating the rare adaptive signals from a potentially large background of non-adaptive genomic change.

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Comparative Methods to Detect Selection on CNV-Grs
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core logic of Phylogenetic Comparative Methods (PCMs) from macroevolution to population genomics. In evolutionary biology, PCMs use phylogenetic trees to control for shared ancestry when testing if trait variation across species is consistent with natural selection versus neutral drift. The analogous solution involves fitting models (e.g., Brownian motion) to trait data on a tree and testing for significant deviations. Adapted to the original idea, we construct a population-level phylogeny or haplotype network for the genomic regions flanking CNVs containing Grs. We then model the expected neutral evolution of CNV-Grs frequencies or patterns along this tree, accounting for population splits and migration. Signatures of selection are detected as statistically significant deviations from this neutral phylogenetic null model—such as accelerated frequency shifts, convergent evolution in independent lineages, or correlations with environmental variables—thereby pinpointing CNV-Grs under recent adaptive selection.

ideas.title: Synthetic Chloroplast Genomes for Targeted Larval Sensory and Metabolic Reprogramming
ideas.description: This research direction combines the engineering of minimal or function-specific synthetic chloroplast genomes with comprehensive larval-stage transcriptomic profiling. The core aim is to design and introduce synthetic chloroplasts into a model insect system to test hypotheses about how plastid-derived metabolites or signals actively shape larval sensory tissue development and gene expression networks, particularly for Gr/Or genes. This allows for a reverse-genetics approach in a non-plant context, where a custom-built organelle genome is used to perturb and interrogate animal developmental pathways. The fusion enables causal experiments to determine if and how specific chloroplast functions can reprogram heterotrophic physiology and sensory capabilities during critical life stages.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic stability of gene order as a tool to trace the evolutionary history of female-biased Gr functional shifts
ideas.description: This research investigates whether the evolutionary history of functional expansion in female-biased gustatory receptors (Grs)—from mediating only oviposition to also regulating feeding or other non-reproductive behaviors—can be reliably traced using the relative stability of their genomic context. By comparing phylogenetic trees built from conserved gene order in chromosomal regions harboring these Grs against trees built from the Gr sequences themselves, we can test if major functional shifts correlate with detectable, lineage-specific micro-rearrangements or are instead superimposed on a stable genomic backbone. This synergy allows us to determine if changes in gene function are driven by, or occur independently of, local genomic instability, providing a new dual-locus (sequence + synteny) approach to linking genotype, genomic architecture, and behavioral phenotype evolution.

ideas.title: Applying Signal Processing Filters to Gene Order Phylogenetics
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that raw gene order is directly suitable for phylogenetic reconstruction by positing that frequent, small-scale rearrangements act as phylogenetic 'noise.' Drawing an analogy from signal processing, where low-pass filters are used to remove high-frequency noise from time-series data to reveal the underlying trend, this research proposes to develop analogous computational filters for gene order data. The adapted idea is to treat lineages or clades as windows in 'genome space' and design algorithms that identify and suppress the signal from frequent, small-scale inversions, transpositions, or duplications. The core test would be to compare phylogenetic trees built from raw gene order data against trees built from filtered data across simulated and empirical datasets with known rearrangement rates, evaluating which approach yields more accurate and robust phylogenies when the history includes both large, rare macro-rearrangements and constant micro-rearrangements.

ideas.title: Resolving the functional interplay of CNV and amino acid variation in chemosensory gene evolution
ideas.description: This project integrates high-confidence, experimentally validated full-length cDNA sequences for chemosensory genes (Grs, Ors) with functional assays to dissect the evolutionary drivers of host specialization. By cloning the precise gene variants from different insect populations or species, we can express them in heterologous systems and measure their response profiles to host-specific volatiles. This allows direct comparison of receptors from expanded gene families (CNV) versus orthologous receptors with amino acid substitutions, testing whether CNV provides novel ligand-binding scaffolds while amino acid changes fine-tune specificity within those scaffolds. The synergy lies in using molecularly confirmed gene models to perform definitive functional experiments that can partition the phenotypic effects of these two major evolutionary mechanisms.

ideas.title: Testing the 'Regulatory vs. Coding' Paradigm in Sensory Evolution
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that amino acid changes in conserved gustatory receptors (Grs) are equally important for host specialization by drawing an analogy from Evolutionary Developmental Biology. In Evo-Devo, the debate over whether morphological evolution was driven by coding changes in toolkit genes or changes in their regulatory elements was resolved by direct comparative functional tests. Adapting this solution, the research would systematically test if copy number variation (CNV) in Gr genes is a primary driver of sensory evolution relative to amino acid changes. Using a model insect system, CRISPR/Cas9 would be employed to create two contrasting experimental genotypes: one altering Gr gene copy number while keeping protein sequences constant, and another introducing specific, hypothesized adaptive amino acid changes while maintaining normal copy number. The host specialization phenotype (e.g., behavioral preference, neural response to host compounds) would then be quantitatively compared between these genotypes to determine the relative contribution of each genetic mechanism, thereby testing the core assumption that coding changes are the primary substrate for sensory adaptation.

ideas.title: Adapting Software Dependency Analysis to Map Plastid Gene Network Evolution
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from software engineering, where tools analyze dependency graphs of software packages to distinguish core required libraries from optional accessories and resolve compatibility conflicts. The analogous solution involves mapping these dependencies to understand ecosystem structure and evolution. We adapt this by treating plastid genomes as biological 'software packages'. The core photosynthetic apparatus genes are analogous to essential dependencies, while accessory genes (e.g., involved in stress response or metabolism) are optional libraries. By applying algorithms from software dependency analysis, we can create a network model of plastid gene content. This model will map the co-evolution, gain, and loss of gene modules across diverse plant lineages, helping to predict functional compatibility between nuclear and plastid genomes and revealing evolutionary constraints on plastid 'package' assembly.

ideas.title: Applying Epidemiological Network Models to Predict Host Plant Diversification from Glucosinolate Gene Duplication
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution methods from epidemiology and network science to the original problem of testing if Gr duplication rates predict host plant diversity. In the analogous field, network models predict how pathogen traits influence outbreak spread and strain diversity across a host contact network. The adapted solution treats glucosinolate (Gr) biosynthetic gene clusters as 'chemical traits' that mediate plant-herbivore interactions and constructs a simulated network of potential host plant niches. Using an agent-based or compartmental model, it simulates how different Gr duplication 'strains' colonize and drive speciation within this niche network. This generates testable predictions about diversification rates as a function of Gr duplication, creating a mechanistic simulation framework to complement and inform the application of phylogenetic comparative methods from macroevolution.

ideas.title: A Core-Pan Framework for Plastid Pangenomes Using Population Genomics Pipelines
ideas.description: This idea proposes to scale up plastid pangenome projects by drawing a direct analogy from the mature field of bacterial pangenomics. The analogous problem—characterizing genomic variation across thousands of individuals within a microbial species—was solved by establishing standardized pipelines for core/accessory genome analysis and pan-genome modeling. The adapted solution is to create a comprehensive pangenome resource for plastids by developing a dedicated computational framework. This framework would process vast numbers of plastid sequences within a species or genus, using orthology clustering to define a core plastome and a variable accessory compartment, ultimately constructing a pangenome graph that captures all observed plastid variation in a structured, analyzable format.

ideas.title: Intra-organellar genome heterogeneity in chemosensory neurons as a modulator of female-specific taste perception
ideas.description: This research direction investigates whether the mitochondrial genomes within the chemosensory neurons expressing female-biased gustatory receptors (Grs) are a homogeneous population or exhibit functional heterogeneity. It hypothesizes that variations in mitochondrial DNA sequence or copy number within these specific neurons could alter cellular energetics and calcium dynamics, thereby modulating the expression or function of the female-specific Grs. The project would characterize both the mitochondrial genomic landscape and the electrophysiological/behavioral responses to candidate ligands in these cells. This synergy explores a previously unexamined link between intra-organellar genetic variation and the neural basis of sexually dimorphic behavior.

ideas.title: A Community-Driven, Extensible Rule Engine for Lineage-Specific Plastid Genome Annotation
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that a single annotation rule set (DOGMA) is sufficient for all plastid lineages by adapting a solution from software engineering. In programming, extensible parser frameworks like ANTLR solve the analogous problem of interpreting diverse and novel syntax by separating a core parsing engine from modular, user-defined grammars. We propose to develop a community-driven, extensible rule engine for plastid genome annotation. The system would consist of a core engine implementing fundamental genomic logic, paired with pluggable, lineage-specific rule sets that define how to identify novel genes, atypical introns, or structural rearrangements unique to particular algal or plant groups. This allows the robust annotation of atypical plastid genomes while providing a standardized framework for sharing and validating annotation protocols across the community.

ideas.title: Validate in silico gene predictions using a high-fidelity long-read sequencing pipeline
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from historical manuscript reconstruction, where a definitive text is created from multiple imperfect copies, to the problem of validating in silico gene predictions. The analogous philological method involves comparing independent manuscript copies to produce a critical edition. Adapted here, the solution is to experimentally generate multiple independent full-length cDNA clones for each predicted gustatory (Gr) or olfactory (Or) receptor gene, sequence them with high-fidelity long-read technology, and computationally compare them to the in silico model. The consensus of these experimental 'biological manuscripts' is used to confirm, correct, or refute the computational prediction, resulting in a validated, authoritative gene model for downstream functional studies.

ideas.title: DOGMA-NEXT: An adaptive, anomaly-aware framework for annotating novel plastid genomes
ideas.description: This project develops a hybrid annotation system that combines a core rule-based classifier (DOGMA-like) with a machine learning module trained to identify and characterize genomic regions that defy existing lineage-specific rules. The system outputs standard annotations where possible, but also generates structured reports on novel open reading frames, atypical structural variants, and potential non-canonical regulatory elements, providing specific targets for experimental validation. This approach ensures robust annotation of known plastid types while systematically detecting and quantifying novelty beyond the limits of pre-defined rules.

ideas.title: Develop a modular, plugin-based software suite for comparative plastid genomics
ideas.description: This research idea proposes to create improved computational tools for plastid genome analysis by drawing an analogy to the modular, community-driven software frameworks common in astrophysics (e.g., IRAF, Astropy). The analogous solution involves a core library with standardized data structures and fundamental operations, coupled with a plugin architecture that enables the research community to develop and share specialized modules. Adapted to genomics, the project would involve developing a core software suite for basic plastid genome handling (assembly, annotation, alignment) and a well-defined application programming interface (API). This framework would allow third-party researchers to easily create, distribute, and integrate plugins for analyzing plastid genome repeats, gene content, and evolutionary patterns in a standardized, interoperable environment, accelerating tool development and methodological comparison.

ideas.title: Single-cell chloroplast genomics to resolve functional Gr expression in adult plant cells
ideas.description: This project combines single-cell chloroplast genome sequencing with single-cell transcriptomic and functional assays for gustatory receptors (Grs) in adult plant cells. By isolating individual cells from mature tissues, we can sequence their chloroplast genomes to identify mutations, heteroplasmy, or copy number variations. Simultaneously, we profile the expression and functional status of Grs in the same cell, testing whether 'expressed but non-functional' Grs correlate with specific chloroplast genomic or metabolic states. This reveals if organellar function directly regulates or is signaled by the functional deployment of chemosensory pathways in differentiated cells.

ideas.title: Modeling plastid repeat evolution using citation network dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts methods from bibliometric citation network analysis to model the evolution of dispersed repeats in plastid genomes. In scientometrics, the flow and clustering of ideas are mapped by treating papers as nodes and citations as directed edges, identifying hubs and tracing conceptual diffusion. Here, we treat each dispersed repeat instance as a node and establish directed edges based on sequence homology, shared flanking regions, or inferred evolutionary relationships. By applying network theory metrics—such as centrality to find key, highly connected 'hub' repeats, and community detection algorithms to find groups of co-evolving repeats—we can model how repeat families propagate, reorganize, and drive structural evolution in plastid genomes over time, providing a dynamic systems-level view of repeat evolution.

ideas.title: Single-Cell Plastid Transcriptomics for Chemosensory Receptor Mapping
ideas.description: This project fuses plastid pangenomics with single-cell RNA-seq to investigate how natural variation in plastid genomes correlates with gene expression programs in specialized chemosensory cells. We will first construct a plastid pangenome for a model species (e.g., a plant or alga with defined chemosensory structures) to catalog all organelle genomic variation. We will then perform single-nucleus or single-cell RNA-seq on chemosensory tissues, simultaneously profiling nuclear transcripts (like Gr/Or receptors) and plastid transcripts. This integrated approach will uncover whether specific plastid haplotypes or expression states are associated with distinct chemosensory neuron types or functional states, providing a holistic view of how cytoplasmic genetic variation influences cellular sensory specialization.

ideas.title: Functional Profiling of Chemosensory Neuron Transcriptomes Across Life Stages Using Genetic Intersectional Strategies
ideas.description: This project moves beyond descriptive expression profiling by integrating cellular resolution and functional genetics. We employ an intersectional genetic strategy to isolate and sequence the transcriptomes of cells that are both neuronal and express Gr/Or family genes, specifically in larval tissues and non-canonical adult sensory organs. This approach filters out non-neuronal expression and developmental noise, focusing resources on populations with a high probability of being bona fide chemosensory neurons. The resulting dataset directly links receptor expression to neuronal identity, providing a robust foundation for functional validation and circuit mapping.

ideas.title: Functional Deorphanization of Female-Biased Gustatory Receptors via High-Throughput Chemical Screening and Behavioral Profiling
ideas.description: This project adapts the established 'deorphanization' pipeline from pharmacology and drug discovery, where orphan GPCRs are systematically matched with their ligands, to the problem of characterizing female-biased gustatory receptors (Grs) in Drosophila. The analogous solution involves high-throughput screening of chemical libraries in heterologous expression systems to identify receptor agonists/antagonists, followed by in vivo validation using genetic models. Adapted to this context, the research will first determine the specific ligands for female-biased Grs by expressing them in cell-based systems and screening against a vast panel of potential tastants. Subsequently, the behavioral roles of these ligand-receptor pairs will be elucidated by creating and testing female-specific Gr knockout flies in assays for feeding, oviposition, and courtship, thereby linking molecular function to female-specific physiology and ecology.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporal Atlas of Gustatory Receptor Expression Across Development
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from developmental neurobiology's creation of spatiotemporal brain atlases, this project adapts the solution of systematic, stage-specific transcriptional profiling across microdissected tissues. The goal is to expand transcriptome analysis beyond adult sensory appendages by constructing a comprehensive spatiotemporal atlas of Gustatory and Olfactory Receptor (Gr/Or) expression. This will involve performing RNA sequencing on larval stages and other non-canonical tissues (e.g., wings, ovipositor, internal organs) across multiple developmental timepoints. The adapted approach will reveal the complete developmental trajectory of chemosensory receptor deployment, identify tissues with covert chemosensory functions, and uncover potential life-stage-specific roles for these receptors in processes like feeding, navigation, and reproduction.

ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea first uses existing Heliconius transcriptomic and single-cell sequencing data from antennae and proboscises to identify genes consistently co-expressed with putative gustatory receptors. Candidate co-receptors and chaperones are then cloned and systematically co-expressed with orphan Heliconius Grs in a heterologous cell system (e.g., Xenopus oocytes or Drosophila S2 cells). This creates a tailored expression platform that more accurately replicates the native signaling environment, thereby increasing the likelihood of functional receptor expression and reducing false negatives during ligand screening. The final pipeline validates receptor-ligand pairs using both the tailored heterologous system and Heliconius tissue electrophysiology.

ideas.title: Inverse Problem: Design Genomes from Functions via Analogical Circuit Synthesis
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from Electronic Design Automation, specifically High-Level Synthesis (HLS). In EDA, HLS tools solve the inverse problem of generating an optimal hardware circuit layout from a high-level functional specification. We adapt this solution to chloroplast genome engineering. The goal is to create a computational framework—a 'Biological High-Level Synthesis' tool—where inputs are quantitative functional targets (e.g., photosynthetic efficiency, biosynthetic pathway flux, resilience parameters) and design constraints (e.g., genome size, codon usage, regulatory element compatibility). The framework would leverage databases of known chloroplast genes, promoters, and regulatory sequences, combined with models of their interactions, to algorithmically generate and simulate candidate minimal or engineered chloroplast genomes. This approach systematically inverts the design process, moving from desired phenotypic functions to proposed genomic implementations, thereby rigorously testing evolutionary and functional hypotheses about chloroplast organization and enabling the rational design of advanced photosynthetic systems.

ideas.title: Robust detection of selection on CNV regions via integrated copy-number-aware population genetics and neutral simulation framework
ideas.description: This redesigned approach creates a robust pipeline for detecting natural selection acting on copy number variant (CNV) regions containing Grs. It begins with a specialized data processing stage using validated CNV genotyping and phasing tools to produce accurate, population-scale copy number matrices. Selection detection then employs novel statistics designed for multi-allelic CNV data, moving beyond traditional SNP-centric methods. Finally, the core innovation is a comprehensive neutral simulation framework that models CNV mutation dynamics and demography to generate precise null distributions, enabling rigorous statistical calibration and the separation of true selective sweeps from confounding signals.

ideas.description: This research direction applies network theory, used to model dispersed repeats in genomes, to single-cell RNA-seq data profiling chemosensory receptor (Gr/Or) expression. Instead of analyzing sequence repeats directly, the expression patterns of paralogous receptor genes across thousands of individual neurons are modeled as co-expression or similarity networks. This allows the inference of evolutionary constraints and innovation by identifying tightly connected 'modules' of co-expressed receptors that may have arisen from recent duplications or selection for coordinated function. The synergy reveals how repeat-derived gene family expansion is functionally wired at the single-cell level to generate perceptual coding capacity.

This approach directly tests whether CNV effects are independent, necessary, or sufficient compared to AA changes, overcoming the correlation and epistasis problems inherent in comparative genomics. The result is a causal map of how discrete genetic changes drive sensory evolution.

These visual observations will be rigorously validated using complementary, native-state biochemical and biophysical methods on rapidly processed samples. The integrated data will establish whether linear or branched forms are genuine biological structures, revealing their potential roles in replication, repair, or genome management within the plastid.

ideas.title: Functional Profiling of Expressed Gustatory Receptors via Metabolic and Signaling Flux Analysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that all expressed gustatory receptors (Grs) in adults are functional. Drawing an analogy from enzymology, where metabolic flux analysis distinguishes active enzymes from inactive homologs, we adapt this solution to chemosensation. The adapted research idea involves functional profiling of expressed Grs by measuring 'signaling flux' in response to ligand presentation in adult Drosophila. By combining controlled tastant stimulation with real-time readouts of neuronal activity (Ca2+ imaging) and behavior, alongside molecular assays for receptor localization and coupling, we can identify which Grs actively participate in adult gustation versus those that are expressed but physiologically silent, potentially having non-functional roles or functions limited to development.

ideas.title: Circuit-level dissection of female-biased Gr function across competing behavioral states
ideas.description: This study directly tests the functional specificity of female-biased gustatory receptors (Grs) by determining if their underlying neural circuits are dedicated to oviposition or multiplexed for other behaviors. We use a closed-loop behavioral arena where female Drosophila sequentially perform feeding, egg-laying, and exploration, while simultaneously monitoring activity in Gr-expressing neurons via a miniaturized microscope. Crucially, we apply state-specific optogenetic activation or silencing during each behavior to see if manipulating these neurons disrupts one state without affecting the others, thereby establishing causal links and revealing dedicated versus shared circuit logic.

ideas.title: Adapt single-cell mass cytometry from immunology to map chemosensory receptor neuron types
ideas.description: The analogous problem in immunology was the need to classify dozens of immune cell subsets based on their surface protein expression, solved by developing mass cytometry (CyTOF). This method uses metal-tagged antibodies and time-of-flight detection to measure over 40 parameters per cell without spectral overlap. Adapting this solution, we propose using CyTOF to profile chemosensory tissues. By employing a panel of metal-tagged antibodies targeting Gr and Or receptor proteins, along with canonical neuronal markers, we can directly quantify receptor protein co-expression at single-cell resolution. This enables the high-dimensional classification of olfactory and gustatory neuron types based on their actual protein expression landscape, providing a complementary and potentially more functionally relevant map than transcriptomic inference alone.
